codewordLabels.TIDE-NAME: you said your own true name
codewordLabels.TIDE-SHORE: a plain thing of the waking shore
codewordLabels.TIDE-LULLED: you let the night hold you
codewordLabels.TIDE-STILL: you let the stillness all the way in
codewordLabels.TIDE-INGENUE-FREED: you kept her above the water
codewordLabels.TIDE-BOAT: the ladder up works both ways
codewordLabels.TIDE-WITNESSED: you saw the morning that never came

blocks.s: You come up the gangway out of the cold off the water, and the cold ends the moment the deck takes your weight; one step you are in the wind off the black sea, the next you are not, and the line between is sharp as a doorsill. The boat deck is all warm light and white enamel and brass gone soft gold under the deck-lamps, teak underfoot scrubbed pale, and somewhere far below your feet a band is playing, the music coming up through the planking more felt than heard. The sea is flat and black and goes out forever on every side with no horizon you can find. There is salt in the air, and coal-smoke, and the band.

You have made this crossing before. You did not stumble aboard lost; you came back, on purpose, because there is a morning you ran from once and a person you stopped being on it, and you mean to find them tonight, before the night that holds this ship lets go.

You go to lift your cuff and check the hour, the old habit, and there is nothing on your wrist. No watch. Or there is a watch, you cannot afterward be sure, but its hands stand at twenty past two and do not move while you look at them. You put your arm down.

lines: THE CURATOR, low and courteous, from somewhere up by the dark of the bridge, before any officer is at the rail:

Welcome back aboard. She rode out lovely tonight, did she not. I made this crossing once myself, you know; I never did reach the far shore. Do come up out of the cold; the cold was never your weather.

choices.label: Go to the purser at the head of the gangway
Hang back at the rail and read the ship first

gated.aside: Ah. You. You did the hard thing once, you let a thing be taken and you stood there while it went, and look at you, back up my gangway with the salt still on you. A passenger who can let go has earned a rest from the letting go. Come aboard. Nothing is taken from anyone, here.

You held on. You always have, in every house, past the hour when holding was wise. The company has been short a hand who knows how to keep a night past its last bar. Come up out of the cold. You are practically aboard already.

You walked out into the daylight once, as your own plain self, and you have learned by now what the cold morning costs, and how little it loves you back. I have the night before that morning, and I have kept it warm. You never have to step into the light again.

You kept the part, last house. You chose the stopped perfect forever and you loved it. I know your kind aboard within a step of the gangway. You will want the deck chair on the sheltered side. You will not want to be woken.

You think you refused the wager, last house. You did not. That was a passage between ports. This is the longest leg of it, and she does not land, and the band has been holding the same bar against your coming aboard.

The warmth of the deck reaches you before the purser does, the moment your weight comes onto the planking, and you have learned in some crossing you cannot quite name that a welcome that fast is a welcome with a hook set in it.

blocks.s: The purser is at your side as you set the cup down, and he takes you in toward the door himself, unhurried, a hand not quite at your elbow, the click of his watch-case sounding once for company. "This way. Mind the sill." The passage runs amidships in white enamel and brass, stateroom doors down both sides, each standing a little open on a lamp turned low and a berth turned down, every cabin warm and waiting and smelling of clean linen and a little of the sea.

"She is a kind ship, for those that let her be kind," he says as you go, and there is something underneath the courtesy that is not quite selling it to you. "I have walked a great many passengers down this passage. Most of them turn in at one of these doors and do not come out, and they are happy, by every sign a man can read, and I close the door soft and go back up for the next." The watch-case clicks. "I will walk you to the head of the stair, if you will keep your feet on the carpet and not turn in. That is as far as my post takes me. After the stair you are the company's, and the company goes down."
The head of the grand staircase is ahead, warm light pouring up it, the band turning over below.

choices.label: Keep your feet on the carpet to the head of the stair
Turn in at one of the warm open doors

blocks.s: The boat deck runs the long length of her under the deck-lamps, warm and bright and very quiet, the band coming up through the planking from somewhere far down inside. The deck holds you in a heat that comes from everywhere and nowhere, and no one hurries you along it.

The lifeboats hang out over the side the whole way aft, swung out on their davits and never lowered, the dry ropes flaked at their feet. Forward stands the deck clock by the wheelhouse ladder, lit, stopped. Aft, from a lit deckhouse with a wireless aerial strung above it, comes a faint regular tapping, on and on, that you find you can almost read. A stair goes down off the after end of the deck toward the music, and from somewhere on it a girl is humming, three notes, rising, over and over. And there is a deckhouse door, padded and warm, standing a little ajar on gold light and the held breath of the band, the route inboard.

choices.label: Go to the lifeboat swung out over the side
Go forward to the stopped clock by the wheelhouse ladder
Go aft to the tapping in the wireless room
Go down the after stair toward the humming and the band
Look in at a lit porthole in the deckhouse
Stop at the brass telegraph by the ladder
Go down the ladder to the well deck where a man is working
Go to the warm door inboard

blocks.s: "What for." Seventy. He cracks the knuckle, eyes on the line going down. "Habit. A bosun sounds. It is what the hands are for. Stand at a rail, pay out the lead, call the marks, and do not think about the man on the bridge nor the lever below that seals her, because if I think on the lever I start to want to throw it. Eighty." He says the next to the water. "That is the trouble with a big man and a quiet ship. She found a thing in me that likes a door shut hard and a thing held down, and she built me a watch where that is the whole of the job. I keep my hands on the line instead. Ninety."

He tips his head up the ladder without looking at you, the nod of a man staying at his post on purpose. "Go up. Mind the big quiet ones below, when you come down to us. A hundred. We are the ones still deciding whether to throw the switch."

choices.label: Leave him to his sounding and go back up

gated.aside: He cracks the one knuckle and the count runs on under his breath, and you know that count from the inside, the arithmetic a body does to keep its hands busy so the rest of it does not have to feel the deck tilt.

blocks.s: You close your hand around the steel watch and put it in your own pocket, and you can feel it going there against your hip, ticking, ticking, a small machine that does not know it is supposed to have stopped. You go back toward the warm with it, and the warm meets you a degree cooler than it left you.

You understand the trade well enough. A passenger who carries a watch that runs is a passenger who can tell, at any moment, that time is passing, that the night is a held thing and not the natural state of the world. The deck likes you measurably less for it. The lamps do not dim; they only seem to lean their warmth somewhere other than where you are standing.
You put your thumb on the watch in your pocket and feel it tick. As long as it ticks, you can count.

choices.label: Walk the boat deck
Go to the warm door inboard

gated.aside: Keep the watch that still runs. When the calm comes, and it will, it will tell you that you are safe. The second hand will tell you the truth, which is that it is twenty past two and has been for a very long time, and safe is only the word the deep uses for drowned.

blocks.s: You step off the sill into the warm passage, but not down the stair, not yet; there is a cabin door standing open on the first warmth, a stateroom, the bunk turned down, a lamp lit low, and you will only sit a moment. You have been on your feet since the dock. You will gather yourself, get your wits back, and go down the grand staircase properly instead of stumbling down it tired and raw.

The bunk takes you in, soft and total. The lamp is exactly the warmth of a room where someone is meant to sleep. On the little shelf at the bunk-head a card lies, your card, with the coffee-ring and the real date, and beside it in a steward's hand a line you do not remember being written: berth B 64, the run, indefinite. The cabin is amidships, low in her, where the roll would be least if she rolled, which she does not.

The stair is right there through the door. You will take it in a moment. The bunk is so very kind, and the lamp is going down of its own accord, not out, only down, lowered as a lamp is lowered in a room where someone is sleeping and must not be disturbed, and a turned-down berth in a ship where the morning never comes is a passage like any other, and you have taken it, and you do not get up.

A steel ladder takes you down to the well deck forward, out of the deck-lamps into a working dark, cargo derricks stowed, hatch covers battened, the cold off the water harder down here where the warm rooms do not reach. A big man is at the rail with a coil of line in his hands, paying it out hand over hand into the black water, sounding for a bottom, and as it runs he is counting, low, under his breath, and cracking one knuckle between the numbers.

"Ten," he says, to the line, not to you. "Twenty. No bottom. Thirty." He does not look round. "Forty. There is no bottom under her. There never is. I sound her every watch the same and the lead goes down and down and the marks run out and there is no bottom and nothing comes up. Fifty." He cracks the knuckle. "You are not meant down here. Go up to the warm. Sixty. Go on."
The line runs out over the rail into water that does not move, mark after mark, fathom after fathom, into a deep that gives back nothing.

choices.label: Ask him what he is sounding for, if there is no bottom
Go back up the ladder to the boat deck

blocks.s: Along the sheltered side of the deckhouse a row of deck chairs is set out, teak and canvas, each with a folded rug across it and a small card slotted in a brass holder on the arm giving the name of the passenger who has the chair for the crossing. The names are good old names. The rugs are squared off. Not one chair is empty, though not one of them holds anyone you can see; the canvas of each is dented in the middle, sagged into the shape of a body, warm where you put your hand to it, as if whoever has the chair has only just got up and will be back directly to lie down again.

The card on the nearest chair gives a name and a berth. The card on the next gives a name and a berth. You go down the row reading them, and near the end there is a card with your berth on it, B 64, and the name above it is not the name you would give, and the canvas is dented warm and waiting, the rug turned back as if a steward has readied it for you against your coming up chilled off the water.

choices.label: Sit in the chair with your berth on it
Leave the row of chairs and walk the deck

blocks.s: A steward is at your elbow before you hear him come, white-jacketed, glad, with a tray, and on the tray a cup in a gimballed holder, steaming, the smell of it good and meaty and warm, beef bouillon against the cold off the water.

"You will have come up chilled, sir, madam," he says, the way stewards say it, certain you will. "Nothing for it like a hot cup. Take it by the rail; the night air is bracing once you have something warm in you. There is no hurry. There is all the night in the world."

The cup is right there. You can feel how much your hands want it, how much the cold off the black water has got into you, how easy it would be to take the warm and the rail and let the night settle. Or you can keep your hands where they are, on your feet, and tell the steward you are not cold and you mean to keep the hour, and stay on your edge.

choices.label: Take the warm cup and the rail
Keep your hands to yourself and keep counting the hours

10.title: The Boat That Was Never Lowered

blocks.s: You go to the nearest lifeboat. It hangs out over the side on its davits, swung out and ready and never lowered, a clinker-built thing under a laced grey tarpaulin, and the davit ropes are flaked down dry on the deck at your feet, the coils stiff, the manila gone the colour of bone. You put a hand to a rope and it is hard as a root; nothing has ever run through these blocks. The boat has hung here, ready, the whole of the held night.

The tarpaulin is laced down with a life-jacket tape, and there is a knot in it you cannot work loose, a hard wet knot some steward pulled tight long ago and someone since has worried at and given up on. Under the loose corner of the canvas you can see into the boat: thwarts, a baler, a folded oilskin coat on the bottom boards with a scorched cuff, and tucked under the gunwale a folded paper.

choices.label: Work the folded paper out from under the gunwale
Reach in for the folded oilskin coat
Look down the deck at someone standing at the far rail
Go to the row of deck chairs along the deckhouse
Leave the boat and walk the deck

11.title: The Table You Cannot Rise From (an ending)

blocks.s: You go down the after stair too fast, past the girl who is not on it now, down toward the music because the music is warm and the deck was so cold, and the stair lets you out not at the grand staircase but in the warm rooms themselves, the lit saloon, the band on its dais turning over the waltz, the tables laid white and full of unhurried evening dress. A steward has a chair out for you at a table by the band before you have chosen one, and the chair is the right give, and the table is laid for you, your card propped against the cruet with the coffee-ring upward, B 64.

You sit, only to listen a moment, only until you have your breath, and the band is good, and the room is warm, and the chair holds you as the deck chair would have, as the bunk would have, as everything warm on this ship holds you once you stop standing. There was a stair you meant to go down. A water at the foot of it. A person. You hold the thought and the band turns over into the same waltz and the warmth closes over the thought gently, and you stop, somewhere between one held bar and the next held bar that is the same bar, being able to find your feet, or the reason you would want them, and you are still there at the table by the band when the morning does not come, seated, served, warm, and unable to rise, applauding a waltz that has never once reached its end.

"How do I remember." The punch clicks. "On purpose, against the grain of the place. She runs smoother on a crew that has forgotten every face it ever boarded; a man who keeps count is a man who notices she is not going anywhere. I keep a thing in my coat to remember by, and I will not tell you what, because it is mine, and a man on this ship ought to have one thing the manifest does not list."
He folds his hands again on the ledger.

"I will tell you only this. There was a child used to stand at the rail of the boat deck, an age ago, before your crossing, watching the water that never came up, asking the same small question over and over, and no one ever did answer it. I remember the question. I keep it where I keep the rest." The courtesy comes back down over the tired thing like a tarpaulin pulled to. "The card, when you are ready. Mind the sill."

choices.label: Give him your boarding card
Refuse to be boarded at all

blocks.s: You ask what he keeps sending, if no shore answers, and he taps it out and says it with the tapping, half a beat ahead of your asking.

"...if no shore answers. The same word. The position. Where she is, which is here, which is the same here it was the last time I sent it and the time before that." The key goes on under his hand. "You learn the set so well you forget you ever did not know it. I have made this crossing more times than the wire has dots in it, and somewhere along the way I mislaid which shore I first sent from. A long enough run does that. You learn everyone's traffic and you forget your own call-sign was ever yours."
He taps. The same word, over and over, into a sea with no shore listening.

choices.label: Ask him which shore he first sent from
Ask him about the one who keeps this ship
Ask him the way down to the music and the one you came for
Go back on deck

blocks.s: Down the long deck, at the far aft rail where the deck-lamps give out toward the dark, there is someone standing. They stand the way you stand, weight on one hip, head a little down, and the coat on them is the cut of your coat, and even at this distance you can see the collar is turned under wrong on the one side, the side you have never in your life turned it. They are looking out at the black water, or they are looking at you; from here you cannot tell, and the not telling is worse than either.

You take a step toward them and they do not turn. You take another and the deck seems to put more deck between you, the rail no nearer. When you look away, to find your footing on a ringbolt, and look back, there is only a folded oilskin laid over the davit-arm, and a steward straightening it with a fond hand, and the steward lifts his face to you, glad, and it is an ordinary steward's face, and you cannot say why your own breath has gone short.
The oilskin on the davit has a scorched cuff. You did not, you are fairly sure, leave it there.

choices.label: Go to the davit and the oilskin
Leave the far rail and walk the deck

gated.aside: The one at the far rail wears your coat with the collar turned under on the side you would never turn it, and dry, bone dry, in all that wet, and you of all of them know the particular cold of watching something wear you better than you ever managed.

blocks.s: You ask the way down to the band, to the person you came back for, and he answers from the key, the word arriving a half-beat before your question lands.

"...the one you came for. Down. It is always down on this ship. In through the deckhouse door, down the grand staircase past the stopped clock at the foot of it, into the lit rooms where the waltz is, and down from there, deck by deck, to where the water stands." The key ticks. "She will let you go down; she does not mind your going down. What she minds is your going down still counting the hours. Keep something that runs, keep your own watch, and the stair is steeper and colder. Let her warm you and stop counting, and the stair is easy, and you do not come back up it. The door is amidships. You knew that. I only said it first, the way I say everything first."

choices.label: Go to the warm door inboard
Walk the deck a while longer

blocks.s: "What do I want." She turns it over, deciding whether you can be trusted with it. "I want a morning. An ordinary, rotten morning, with the sun coming up grey over a wet dock and everything still to be lost, and me getting older toward it. Here there is no morning. It is always twenty past two and the water never comes up and nobody ever quite goes under, and they call that being safe." She tugs the frock. "It is not safe. It is just stopped. I would rather drown getting somewhere than float here forever getting nowhere."
She stops, having said more than she meant, and does not take it back.

"You came back for something. The returning ones always have the look. Whatever they offer you down there, keep your own count of the hours. I keep mine penciled where the ship cannot see it." Then, dry, the armour back up: "Go on, then. You will want to be down past the band before someone charming comes up to walk you there."

choices.label: Promise her nothing, but mean it; go back on deck

gated.aside: Three rising notes come up the after stair, shaking a little at the top, and you know them from the floor of you, the sound you make when you are afraid and will not say so. She is down there refusing to go still, and you understand the refusing all the way down.

blocks.s: Tucked into the laced corner of the tarpaulin, jammed there by a thumb years ago, is a torn half of a third-class ticket. Just the half: a port of embarkation you can read, a destination you can read, a fare in shillings, and the tear straight through the middle of someone's printed name. A real place, a real fare, an ordinary crossing somebody bought and meant to make and never finished.

You could leave it laced in the canvas with the rest of the boat's dead kit. Or you could keep it on you, a torn stub of the waking world, a place with a name on it that exists in the daylight, ashore, where the clocks run.

You close your hand on it. The deck notices. It is nothing you could name: the warmth backs off your shoulders by a degree it would deny, and the band, far below, seems to lose you for a bar, the way a host's smile slips when they catch you pocketing the matchbook to remember the night by. You have made yourself a little harder to keep.

choices.label: Reach in for the oilskin coat with the scorched cuff
Walk the deck

blocks.s: You step back to where the gangway came aboard and there is no gangway now, only a gap in the rail with a chain hooked across it and the black water far below, flat and unhurried, giving back the row of lit portholes in long unbroken smears. The dock is not there. You do not remember it leaving. It is simply not there, and you cannot find the moment it stopped being there, and after a little you stop trying to.
The water does not lap at the hull. It stands against her, level and still, at one fixed mark on the plating, and does not climb and does not fall.

The purser is at your shoulder, not having seemed to cross the deck. "She is under way," he says, which is plainly not true; there is no wake, no throb of the engines you can feel, no wind of passage. "Best come in. The deck is no place to stand about, this time of the morning." The punch clicks. "Mind the sill."

choices.label: Walk the boat deck a while yet
Let him take you in toward the warm and the music

blocks.s: You work the folded paper out. It is a boat-list, the kind pinned up in a companionway: this boat's number, its capacity, and a column of berth numbers assigned to it, the souls who were to come to this boat if ever the order were given. The order was never given. The paper is soft as cloth at the folds.

Half the column is smeared under a tide-mark where damp got at it once and dried. The numbers that survive you read down, and your berth is among them, B 64, in a clerk's neat hand, with a date beside the heading that is the date off your card, the morning you ran. Someone went down the list in pencil long ago, a small tick against each berth, accounting for the souls one by one. Your berth is not ticked. There is a single pencil stroke drawn beside it instead, the kind of stroke a person makes when they have not yet decided whether to tick or to cross.
Under the loose canvas the folded oilskin coat lies on the bottom boards, the scorched cuff showing.

choices.label: Reach in for the oilskin coat with the scorched cuff
Pocket a stub of the ordinary world from the boat
Fold the list away and walk the deck

blocks.s: You go aft to the lit deckhouse under the aerial, and the tapping gets louder and more regular until you are at the door of the wireless room: a small warm cabin, a brass-and-bakelite set on a shelf, a key under a man's hand. He sits with his back half to you, headphones cocked off one ear, and he is tapping, on and on, and as you come to the doorway he speaks without turning, flat, finishing the thought you had not finished forming.

"...not finished forming, no," he says. "You do that. You think your next word a half-beat ahead of yourself and then you are startled to hear it in another man's mouth. I have been tapping the same traffic so long I send it before the hand wants to. Sit, if you like. The set still works. There is no shore answering, but the set works."

choices.label: Ask him what he is sending, if no shore answers
Ask him about the one who keeps this ship
Leave him to his key and go back on deck

blocks.s: You pick up the tin sailboat off the thwart. It weighs nothing. It is a penny toy off a barrow, pressed tin, the little sail soldered on crooked, and the keel is worn down to bright metal where a thumb rode it back and forth, back and forth, the small hard worry of a child carried somewhere they did not want to go, holding the one thing of their own very tight.

There is no child here. There is a scorched coat and a watch and a tin boat in a lifeboat that was never lowered, and a name torn off a ticket, and a berth on a list with one undecided pencil stroke beside it.
You put the boat back in the breast pocket where the steward kept it, because it is the steward's, or the child's, and not yours, and you fold the coat down again the way it was folded.

choices.label: Take the watch from the coat instead, before you go
Leave the coat and walk the deck

blocks.s: You stand at the foot of the wheelhouse ladder and look up it, into the dark, where the bridge runs across the head of the ship. There are no lights up there. The wheelhouse windows give back only the deck-lamps below, a row of small warm reflections in black glass, and behind the glass nothing moves.

And yet the bridge is not empty. You are as sure of it as you have been of anything since the gangway. There is a stillness up there with shoulders to it, a presence at the forward rail that is not watching the sea, because there is nothing in the sea, but watching the deck, watching the company come aboard, weighing each one. It does not call down. It does not need to. It has the patience of a thing that decides who is landed and who is kept, and is in no hurry, because the hour will not change while it makes up its mind.

You do not climb the ladder. The cold up there is a different cold from the cold off the water; the water's cold is only cold, and this is the cold of being looked at and not yet judged. You go back down the deck, and you feel the bridge follow you with its stillness the whole way.

choices.label: Go to the stopped clock at the head of the ladder
Walk back along the boats

23.title: Why She Stays on the Stair

blocks.s: "Why won't I go down." She laughs, short, nothing warm in it. "Because down there it is so nice. That is the whole trouble. It is warm and the band is good and there is a steward for every want you have, and you take the chair and the cup and the waltz and you stop minding that the clock has stopped, and after a while you stop minding anything at all. They have a chair ready for me. Everyone keeps telling me how I have earned a sit-down."
She picks at the lent frock's shoulder.

"I am the newest aboard. You can tell the newest. We still keep our own count of the hours. Give it long enough, they say, and I will lose the count and not even want it back, and they say it like it is the good news."
She hums the three notes once, quick, and at the top they shake, just a little, and that is the sound she makes when she is frightened and will not let it show.

choices.label: Ask what she wants, if not the warm
Ask what she keeps humming
Go back up on deck

blocks.s: You want to be sure of the time, so you say it aloud to the steward, the hour off the stopped clock, twenty past two, and then, before you have thought better of it, the rest of it: the date, the year, the year it ought to be ashore where the clocks run.

The steward's gladness goes out like a snuffed wick. He does not step back; he simply stops being warm, the way a room stops being warm when a door is opened on the night. "We do not say the year aboard, sir, madam," he says, quiet, almost frightened for you. "Not out loud. The bridge hears a year said aloud. It marks the one who knows what year it is, the one who came up out of the running time and remembers the dock." He glances up at the dark of the wheelhouse. "You have marked yourself now. You are one of the awake ones, the ones she has to work at. I would go in and go down quietly, if I were you, before the cold up there has a good look at you."
You have an edge on you now you cannot put down. The hour is a real number. You know what it ought to be ashore. The night cannot quite pretend otherwise to you any more.

choices.label: Go to the door inboard, marked and awake
Walk the deck while your edge holds

blocks.s: You cannot do it tonight. You turn back from the sill, away from the warm stair and the band and the long way down, out to the rail in the cold, just for a little, just until your legs are steady and the stair stops being so far down and so bright. You have the boat-list folded in your hand, the torn ticket with its real port and its real fare, the half-remembered shore, and you stand at the cold rail and you almost have it, the name of the place, the morning, the person you came to be again.

But the cold is hard, and the water below is very far and very still, and the warm is right there through the door, and you tell yourself you will go down tomorrow, when you are rested, when you are more yourself. You fold the ticket away. You go back in toward the warm, not down the stair, only into the gold of the first rooms, where it is kind, and a steward tucks you into a chair and the band turns over and over in a way that is, you find, deeply restful.

Tomorrow. The ship is very good at tomorrow. Up on the after stair a girl hums three rising notes and does not come down to tell you to keep your count, because she has learned that by the time the returning ones are back in the warm and saying tomorrow, they have stopped being able to hear her, and the shore in your pocket goes soft at the folds, unread, a place you will mean to make for, later, when you are less tired.

You ask him which shore he first sent from, before all the crossings, when his call-sign was his own. The key stops. It is the only thing you have asked that he cannot reach ahead and find already sent, and the silence where his tapping was is the loudest thing in the little room.

"...which shore." He turns his face toward you at last, and it is an ordinary tired face, a young man's face worn flat. "I have looked for it the way you look for a word that has gone, standing with the key in your hand and the shape of it right there and nothing in it. It is gone. I have sent so many positions, for so long, so well, that I cannot now tell you which sea I was first over, or what port had me, or whether I am sending toward a shore or only away from one." He puts the headphone back to his ear. "That is what a crossing that does not land does to a man. You learn the whole sea and you forget you ever stood on the ground."
The set hums. He begins to tap again, the same word, the position, here, here, here.

choices.label: Ask him the way down to the music
Leave him to his key and go back on deck

blocks.s: You reach in under the canvas and draw out the folded oilskin. It is a steward's coat, you can tell by the cut and the cheap warm lining, and it has a real flaw, a scorch up one cuff where someone leaned a sleeve too near a galley flame and singed it brown, the edges gone hard. It smells of cold tallow and salt.

In the breast pocket your fingers find two things. The first is a child's tin sailboat, a penny toy, the kind sold off a barrow, the red paint thumbed clean off the keel where one small hand held it and held it and held it. You set it on the thwart, very carefully, the way you set down a thing that is asleep.

The second is a pocket-watch, a plain steel one, a steward's watch on a worn lanyard. You take it out, and the crystal is whole, and it is ticking. You can feel it ticking against your palm, the small fast pulse of a watch that someone wound, that has not stopped, that is still keeping ordinary running time in a place where every other clock stands at twenty past two.

choices.label: Keep the watch that still runs
Pick up the tin sailboat and look at it
Put the coat back and walk the deck

blocks.s: You ask him about the one who poured the welcome over you off the bridge, the one that calls itself the Curator. The key stops. He does not finish your thought this time; he offers one of his own, which is rare enough in him that you hold still.

"...the Curator," he says. "Older than any of us in the wireless room or the stokehold. Came aboard a crossing of its own, the oldest hands say, fleeing a morning it could not face, and worked itself a passage that does not land. So it keeps everyone else's night so beautifully, sees us all aboard and seats us in the warm, because it has not once had a shore of its own to make for." The key starts again, slow. "Do not stay to be kept. It will be kind to you, and the kindness is the whole of how she works, and it is enough."

choices.label: Ask him the way down to the music
Go back on deck

gated.aside: It taps your next word a half-beat early, flat, spent, and you know that flatness from the inside, the sound a sender makes after it has carried every message but the one that would let it stop.

blocks.s: You let him bring you the second cup. The first was so good, and the cold is still in you somewhere, and there is no hurry, he says, there is all the night in the world, and you find you believe him. The second cup is as good as the first. So is the third. The steward is always there with the next one warm before the last is quite cold, glad, soft-voiced, asking nothing.

Somewhere between the cups the reason you came up the gangway goes quietly out, a galley light switched off at the end of a long watch when everyone has turned in. There was a card with a coffee-ring and a real date on it. There was a morning. There was a person you stopped being and meant to find. It is here somewhere, the reason, you are sure of it, you will take it up again presently, when you have finished this cup, except that there is always another cup, warm in your hands, and the band is so good, and the night is so kind, and you have lost, you notice without minding, any clear sense of why a person would ever want to go anywhere but exactly here, warm, by the rail, with the next cup coming.

A stair goes down off the after end of the boat deck toward the music, brass-railed, carpeted in the red of the warm rooms below, and you go down a few steps because there is someone on it who is not part of the welcome. A young woman, sat on the turn of the stair with her chin on her knees, humming, three notes, rising, the same three over and over. She stops at the sight of you and looks you up and down, frank and unimpressed, too tired to bother with politeness.
"Oh, marvellous," she says. "A returning one. They do love a returning one. First rule of a sinking ship: nobody good ever liked the band."
She is in a first-voyage frock that is too good for her and that she keeps tugging straight at the shoulder, as if it were lent and she means to give it back unmarked.

choices.label: Ask why she will not go down to the music
Ask what she keeps humming
Leave her to it and go back up on deck

blocks.s: You come to the lectern. The purser does not move until a small mechanical click sounds from somewhere about his cuff, a watch-case shut, a ticket-punch thumbed once in his folded hands, and only then does he speak, so that the click is already a half-beat ahead of him.

"Good evening, and welcome aboard." His voice is clipped and old-world, all steamship courtesy worn smooth as the brass. "I have boarded the company for every crossing this ship has made, and I do not forget a face that has come up my gangway. I shall want your card, if you please. She does not carry a passenger off her manifest. That is not unkindness; it is only the rule of the sea."
He turns up an open ledger on the lectern and sets a pen beside it, and waits, perfectly patient, a man who has waited out far more reluctant arrivals than you.

choices.label: Give him your boarding card to be stamped
Ask him what he means, every crossing
Refuse to be boarded at all

blocks.s: By the foot of the wheelhouse ladder stands an engine telegraph, a tall brass column with a dial in its head, the kind the bridge rings down to the engine room to call for speed. The dial is worn bright by a hundred hands, the words around it black on the brass: AHEAD, full and half and slow; STAND BY; and at the bottom of the dial, STOP. The handle is over to STOP, hard over, and left there. Someone rang her to STOP a long time ago and walked away and no one has ever rung her on again.

You put your hand on the cold handle. You could move it. AHEAD is half the dial away, an inch of brass, and you have the sudden plain notion that if you rang her ahead she would have to go somewhere, that the night is only a ship rung to stop and left. You push the handle. It does not move. It is not stiff; it is fixed, set into the brass as if it had always been cast that way, STOP and only STOP.
Under the dial a small brass plate gives her builder's name and a yard number and a launch date, ordinary as a kettle, and you stand there reading it because it is the most real thing on the deck.

choices.label: Go forward to the stopped clock and the bridge above
Leave the telegraph and walk the deck

blocks.s: You find the card in your hand without quite remembering taking it out. It is real cardboard, soft at the corners with handling: a sailing date printed across the top, a class, a berth number, B 64. There is a brown coffee-ring stamped across one corner where someone set a cup down on it in a hurry one ordinary morning and meant to wipe it off and never did. You read the date. You know it in your body before you can place it, the way you know a stair in the dark by the give of it.

The purser takes the card without looking down. The punch bites a half-moon out of one edge with a sound like a small bone, and he sets the card in the ledger and draws his pen through your name in the open book, one stroke, and you cannot afterward say whether he ticked it or crossed it through.

"There. You are boarded." He inclines his head. "She acknowledges you. Welcome to the company." Something behind his eyes settles onto a weight it has carried before. "They are all aboard for you. They have been aboard a long while."
Behind you the gangway draws itself up off the dark water with a long sigh of rope, and the deck takes a small breath, and leans a degree closer.

choices.label: Ask the way through to the warm, and the music
Walk the boat deck before you go in
Look once more at the gangway folding back into the dark

blocks.s: You stand on the sill with the cold at your back and the warm in your face. The deck behind you is genuinely terrible and genuinely the truth: the black water at its fixed mark, the boats swung out and never lowered, the stopped clock, the cold that does not let your breath thin. The passage ahead is genuinely lovely, and that is the worse of the two. Gold light, the band, the stair going down warm and easy toward everything that is waiting.

At the far rail of the boat deck, just for a moment, there is someone standing at your own slight angle, in a coat the cut of your coat, the collar turned under wrong on the one side, the side you would never turn it. They are watching you decide. When you look straight at them there is only a folded oilskin over a davit, and the purser, having drifted there, lifting it and laying it straight with a fond hand.
The stair is still there through the door. The band is still turning over. The cold is still at your back.

choices.label: Turn your back on the cold and go down the grand staircase
Go down the after stair toward the music too fast
You cannot do it tonight; go back out to the rail in the cold

blocks.s: You go forward to the deck clock on its stand by the wheelhouse ladder. It is a fine instrument, a ship's clock in a brass bezel, the face white enamel, the numerals black, lit by its own small lamp so that it can be read from anywhere along the deck. It says twenty past two. The second hand stands a little short of the twelve and does not fall the last inch.

You watch the second hand and wait for it to let go. It does not let go. Up the wheelhouse ladder, on the dark bridge, you have the sense of someone standing at the forward rail, a stillness with shoulders to it, not looking at the sea ahead, where there is nothing, but down the long deck at you. You do not climb the ladder. Something about the stillness up there says it is not to be climbed toward; it is the kind of presence that decides things and would rather you did not give it occasion.
The tin lamp over the clock-face hums faintly. The hand does not fall.

choices.label: Look up the wheelhouse ladder at the dark bridge
Go aft toward the tapping in the wireless room
Walk back along the boats

blocks.s: You ask what she keeps humming, and she shows you the inside of her wrist where the cuff of the lent frock hides it: a row of pencil ticks on the skin, a great many, going up under the sleeve.

"My count," she says. "The hours. I make a mark for every one I am sure has passed, even though the clock says it has not, even though nothing changes. Three notes up, one mark. It is rubbish, I know it is rubbish, the marks do not add up to anything because nothing here adds up. But while I am keeping the count I know the night is a thing being done to me and not just how things are." She pulls the cuff down over the marks. "You have to keep something they did not give you. A count. A name. A watch, if you are lucky enough to find one that still goes. Keep it where they think they can see you keeping it, so they do not go looking for the bit they cannot."
From below, through the carpet, the band turns over into the same waltz again, the same bars, never the last.

choices.label: Ask what she wants, if not the warm
Go back up on deck

blocks.s: There is a row of portholes in the deckhouse, lit gold from within, and you put your eye to the nearest because there is a small shape against the glass on the inside. It is a child. Small, still, stood up on something to reach the porthole, looking out at the black water and the boats that hang and never fall, as if the dark were a thing worth watching all night.

The child does not look at you through the glass. But one hand is pressed flat to it from inside, and in the other is a tin sailboat, the twin of the one in the steward's coat, the red gone off the keel, held up to the glass as if to show it the sea it will never sail. The small mouth moves, twice, the same shape twice, and you read it before you can stop yourself: is it real.

When you shift your weight to see the child's face the angle is wrong, and there is only the gold of an empty companionway beyond the glass, and your own breath fogging the porthole, which is strange, because your breath should hang in this cold and it does not thin and it does not clear.

choices.label: Step back from the porthole and walk the deck

gated.aside: The small face at the glass is the age you stopped being anyone but yourself, before a single morning ever came and took something. You of all of them know what it is to be the one nobody lets grow up, or grieve, only watch through glass.

blocks.s: You stay at the rail a moment and let the deck show you itself. She rides dead level on the black water, not a degree of roll, every porthole down her long flank burning yellow into the dark. The lifeboats hang out over the side on their davits, swung out and ready, and not one of them has ever been lowered; you can see the davit ropes flaked down on the deck, dry, stiff, the coils gone the grey of old rope that has never once run through a block.
Up forward by the wheelhouse ladder a deck clock is mounted on a stand, lit, its face white as a plate. It says twenty past two. You watch the long hand. It does not travel.

At the head of the gangway a man in a purser's dark coat stands very still behind a small lectern, hands folded on it, watching the gangway. He has the look of someone who has stood in that one spot for a great many sailings. He is not watching the sea. He is watching you, and has been, you think, since the dock.

choices.label: Go to the purser at his lectern
Walk aft along the boats toward a clock and a tapping
Look at the lifeboat swung out nearest you

blocks.s: "Every crossing," he says, and the punch clicks once under his thumb. "I work the gangway. I take the cards, I board the company, I see them down to the warm. It does not sound like much of a post. It is rather more of one than it sounds."
He looks at you properly, and the smooth courtesy thins a moment over something tired underneath.

"You came up this gangway before. I stamped your card with my own hand. I will board you again tonight, gladly, if you will let me." The punch clicks. "But I shall want the card first. And mind the sill of the door, when you come to it; she sits a little down by the head, and the deck is not as level inside as it looks from out here."

choices.label: Ask how he remembers, if the night holds everyone still
Give him your boarding card
Refuse to be boarded at all

gated.aside: The watch-case clicks shut under his thumb a beat before his courtesy lands, and for that beat the two of you are simply men who have boarded a great many people onto passages you never got to take yourselves.

40.title: The Passenger No Ship Will Carry (an ending)

blocks.s: You will not give a card. You stand at the lectern with the pen untouched and you tell the purser, plainly, that you did not come up his gangway to be written into any book, that you came for a person and not for a passage, and a person does not need a manifest.

He does not argue. The courtesy does not even thin. "As you wish," he says, and the punch does not click, because there is nothing to punch. "She does not carry a passenger off her manifest. That is not unkindness. It is the rule of the sea. There is the gangway."

You turn, and the gangway is already drawing up off the dark water, the dock sliding back into the black, and there is a moment, one moment, when you could still go down it. You do not take it. The gangway folds back into the rail and the chain hooks across the gap, and the dock is gone, and you are stood at the edge of the deck with the flat black water far below, unmoving, level at its mark. You could be boarded yet. You have only to give a name and a card. You stand at the gap in the rail with the cold coming up off the water and no part on the ship and no shore behind you, the one passenger no ship will carry and no dock will take back, and the water below does not climb to meet you and does not fall away, because the night has nothing it can do with a soul that will neither board nor leave, except to keep it standing at the rail, deciding, for as long as twenty past two will last, which is to say forever.

You come to the deckhouse door amidships, padded and warm, standing a little ajar on gold light and the held breath of the band. Through it a short passage of white enamel and brass leads to the head of the grand staircase, and you can see the first of it, the polished oak treads going down, the soft fall of warm light up them from the rooms below, and you can hear the waltz properly now, turning over and over toward a last bar it never plays.

The purser is at the door before you, holding it, the punch clicking once under his thumb. "This way, if you please. Mind the sill of the door; she sits a little down by the head." He does not crowd you. He only holds the door, and waits, a man who has seen a great many passengers stand on this exact sill and decide.

This is the edge of the cold deck and the open dark. Inboard is all warmth and music and the long stair down. Out here is the cold, the boats that never fall, the watch that still ticks, the count. Going in means leaving the cold open night for the warm rooms, and going down the grand staircase toward the band and the person you stopped being.

choices.label: Step over the sill and go down the grand staircase
Stand on the sill and look back at the cold deck
Step into the warm passage and into the first cabin to gather yourself

blocks.s: You keep your hands to yourself. You tell the steward you are not cold, which is nearly true, and that you mean to keep the hour, which is wholly true, and you go on standing on your own feet on the cold deck with the count running quiet in the back of your head, one, and one, and one, marking the time the stopped clock will not.

The steward's gladness does not quite land. He withdraws the cup, unoffended, with a good servant's smoothness, but the warmth that came aboard with him goes off down the deck a degree cooler than it arrived. You have refused the first kindness. The night marks it; you can feel yourself a little outside the warm now, on your edge, awake, a passenger who is still counting.
Below, the band turns over into the same waltz. You count the bars. You can hear that it never reaches the last one.

choices.label: Say the hour aloud to the steward, to be sure of it
Go to the door inboard, awake
Walk the boat deck while your edge holds

43.title: The Chair That Is Still There (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the deck chair. The steward has it unfolded and the rug laid ready before you have quite agreed to it, angled to the rail and the black water, and you let yourself down into it with the cup still warm in your hands, and it is, you find, exactly the give your back has wanted since the dock. The rug is over your knees. The cold cannot reach you through the warm. The band comes up through the deck, the same waltz, turning, never the last bar, and you stop, after a while, hearing it as a thing that never ends and only as a thing that is pleasant.

There was a reason you came up the gangway. A morning you ran from. A person you stopped being. You hold the thought of them close and careful against the warm cup in your hands, and the night is so warm around it that the thought is hard to feel as a separate thing from the warmth, and you mean to get up and go in and down the stair and find them, in a moment, when you have rested, when the cup is done.

The cup is never quite done. The steward sees to that, glad, attentive, in no hurry, because there is all the night in the world. The deck chair holds you out under the deck-lamps with the rug to your chin, and the boats hang over you on their davits and never fall, and the water below stands at its mark, and in the morning that never comes the chair is still there at the rail, and so are you, warm, kept, the cup at your elbow gone cold at last in its gimballed holder, skinned over, and your hands too settled to lift it.

You take the cup. It is exactly as good as the cold made you hope, and the warmth goes down into you and spreads, and the rail under your forearms is the right height, and the black water below is not menacing now, only vast and quiet and restful, a thing it would be churlish to be afraid of. The steward stands a courteous pace off, glad to have warmed you, in no hurry at all.

The band, far below, eases into you through the deck. Twenty past two is a perfectly good hour. You stop, for a moment, minding that it is always twenty past two. The cold has gone out of your hands and out of the small insisting part of you that was counting, and the night closes over the count gently, the way warm water closes over a held breath.
It is a small thing, this first warmth taken. It is also the first stitch the night puts in you, and you feel it settle.

choices.label: Set the cup down and let him walk you in to the staircase
Set the cup down at last and go to the door inboard
Take the deck chair the steward is unfolding for you
Let him bring you a second cup

blocks.s: You step over the sill, and the deck sits a little down by the head, just as he said, so that the floor of the passage tilts you gently inboard and down, and you go with it. The cold lets go of you slowly, reluctantly, the open dark closing behind you as the padded door swings to, the salt and the coal-smoke thinning out of the air, the band coming up clear and warm and wrong.

The grand staircase opens below you, a great sweep of polished oak and wrought iron going down under a domed light, deck upon deck of it, warm and golden and full. At the very foot of it, far down, a tall clock stands against the panelling, lit, and you do not have to read its face to know what hour it shows. Beside the clock the water lies across the bottom of the stair, black and still and level, standing at one step and not climbing, giving back the dome-light without a ripple. Figures move through the warm rooms off the landings, in evening dress, unhurried, none of them looking at the stair, none of them looking up.
A latch clicks behind you, up at the sill. The purser has come a step after you, not to stop you, only to see.

"Down, then," he says, and there is something in it that might be respect and might be grief. "They mostly do not. They mostly cannot leave the warm deck for the cold stair down. Mind your footing. She sits a little by the head, and it is a long way to the water, and the water is only the start of the way down."
From somewhere on the bridge above and behind, a single pair of hands comes together once, slow, one clap, and stops, the sound a thing makes when it has seen something it means to weigh later.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm and unhurried, rising up the stairwell to meet you:

There you are, on the stair at last. Down to the warm, where the band plays and the night holds and nothing more is ever taken from you. Take your time. We have the whole of the night ahead of us, and so, for as long as you like, do you.

blocks.s: The stair takes you down toward the band, and the held bar, and the stopped clock at the foot of it, and the water that stands at the same step and waits.

You come down the last of the grand staircase, the oak treads giving a little under you, the deck sitting a shade down by the head so that the descent carries you, and you arrive in the warm. It is total, the warm, it comes from the lamps and the carpet and the bodies and the band, and the cold off the open deck is already a thing that happened to someone else.

The foyer opens around you in white enamel and gilt and mirror, doors off it to the lit public rooms, and dead ahead, set against the panelling, a tall clock stands lit by its own small lamp. Its face is white as a plate. It says twenty past two. The pendulum hangs straight down and does not swing.

At the foot of the clock the water lies across the bottom of the foyer in a flat black sheet, two inches of it, no more, standing at one fixed mark on the gilt skirting and not climbing, giving back the chandelier without a ripple. Evening dress moves through the rooms beyond, unhurried, the dancers turning slow somewhere out of sight to a tempo nothing is keeping.
You go to lift your cuff and check the hour. There is nothing there, or there is, and its hands stand where the clock's stand. You put your arm down.
A steward is already at your elbow with a tray.

choices.label: Take the cup of bouillon off the steward's tray
Wave the tray off and keep your hands to yourself
Go to the tall stopped clock against the panelling
Look into the cloak-room mirror off the foyer
Go through to the ballroom and the band
Take the quieter door to the reading room

blocks.s: You cross the foot of the ballroom toward the long gallery that runs off it, a promenade of tall windows down one side, brass-framed, giving onto the black water and the night. They are not portholes so much as a wall of glass, and against the lowest of them, where the sill comes down near the deck, there is a small shape.

It is a child. Small, still, stood up close to the glass, looking out at the flat black water and the lifeboats hung far above and never lowered, as if the dark were a thing worth watching all night. The child does not look round at you. One hand is flat to the glass from inside. In the other is a tin sailboat, a penny toy, the red paint thumbed off the keel, held up to the window as if to show it the sea it will never sail.
The small mouth moves, twice, the same shape twice, and you read it before you can stop yourself: is it real.

choices.label: Crouch and answer the child
Look out the window at what the child is watching
Step back from the gallery and go to the ballroom floor

blocks.s: You come out onto the floor itself, into the turning, and the long-aboard dancer is there, or comes, and takes your hand, but you do not dance, you keep your feet planted on the parquet and you make her stand with you while the others turn around you both, and you ask her the morning. The one she was running from. The one you are.

And she cannot find hers, and so she gives you yours instead, because the floor does not care whose morning it serves up so long as it serves one. She turns your hand over in her cool glove and looks into it as if it were a dance card.
"Here is yours," she says, gently. "You had a dance card too, did you not, the night you came aboard. A real one, with the dances penciled in."

And it is in your hand, suddenly, a stiff little card on a tasselled cord, a printed list of dances down one side and a space against each for a name, and most of the spaces are filled in your own hand, a name written against dance after dance, the same name, a person you were going to spend the whole night with because in the morning you were going to lose them. And the last three dances on the card are blank. You left them blank. You did not write the name against the last three because you knew, writing it, that you would not be there to dance them, that the morning was going to come between you and the end of the card, and you ran rather than reach the dances you had left empty.

choices.label: Read the name penciled on the card
Look at the three blank dances at the end

blocks.s: "Why count." Six. He cracks the knuckle, eyes on the dog-handle. "Keeps my hands busy and my head off the lever. A man stood at a door long enough starts to think about the door. Seven. Starts to think how quiet it would be with it dogged down, how nobody could get at anybody, how safe, how still. That is the ship talking. She found the part of me that likes a thing shut hard and held down and she gave me a door to want to shut. Eight."
He says the next to the steel. "So I count. While I am counting I am only a man at a door and not the thing she wants me to be, which is the man who seals you all in."

Then, dry, between numbers, jerking his chin back toward the foyer and the band: "Same waltz. I have stood this watch four hundred crossings and they have not once got to the end of it. Nine. Still flat."

choices.label: Ask him to let you through, down toward the staterooms
Leave him to his count and go back to the ballroom

blocks.s: You come to the tall doors of the ballroom, glazed and gilt, and through them the warm is gold and the music is whole, and you push through into it.

The room is a long bright box under a domed and frosted skylight, the floor a wide reach of parquet, the band up on a low dais at the far end, and around the walls the small tables with their shaded lamps and the gilt chairs and the evening dress sitting at its ease. Out on the floor the couples turn, slow, formal, a waltz, the gowns going round and the gloved hands held just so, and not one face among the dancers is in any hurry, because the waltz they are turning to has nowhere it is trying to get.

The band plays. You listen, and you can hear it, the thing the Heavy and the Ingenue both told you: it comes up the long phrase toward the close, and the close is right there, the last bar, the resolving chord that lets the dancers stop and bow and have done, and the band reaches the very lip of it and does not play it, and turns the phrase over and begins again. The waltz is held one bar short of its own end, forever.

Across the floor, by the dais, a steward is bringing a long-aboard dancer toward you. And up on the dais the bandleader has turned, and is watching you over the heads of the band, and lifts his baton hand a half-beat before you have decided to look at him.

choices.label: Let the long-aboard dancer come to you
Go up to the bandstand and the bandleader watching you
Cross the floor toward the far gallery of porthole windows
Stop by the girl at the wall waving a steward's tray away

blocks.s: You let go the last of it. There was a waking corner of you keeping a kind of watch, a count, an edge, a small hard ungiving thing that had held since the gangway, and you set it down, and the stillness comes in over the place where it was and lies flat and level and does not move.

Whatever you were holding, the cards in your hand or the card in your pocket, the name on it, the three blank dances, the morning, it is all very far away now and going further, and you do not mind it going, because to mind a thing you have to be counting, and you have stopped counting. The clock is stopped. You are stopped with it. The peace is total and the peace is a kind of death and the two are, you find, here, the same thing, and there is no part of you left awake to tell them apart.

They will not have to seal a door on you, or lower a boat that never lowers, or do anything at all. You have done it yourself, from the inside, more thoroughly than the cold up on the bridge could ever have managed. You sit in the warm with a good hand or a deep chair, perfectly still, at twenty past two, for as long as twenty past two will last, which is to say until the sea itself runs out, which it does not, which is the point, which is what the stillness costs you, and the last thing in you able to know it has gone quiet too.

You sit in the deep chair in the alcove off the half-landing. It takes you in completely, the give of it exactly the give your back has wanted since the dock, and the low lamp throws a small warm circle that holds just you. The floor and the band fall away behind a curtain of warm air. No steward comes. No one needs you to do anything at all.

It would be the easiest thing in the world to stay. Not to be warmed, not to be served, not even to dance, only to sit, and stop, and let the night close over you like still water closing over a held breath. The card is in your pocket with its name and its three blanks, and from this deep in the chair even that is going soft, going from a thing you carry to a thing you once carried.

The cold stair is just past the curtain of warm. You could get up. The chair does not stop you. It only makes getting up a thing you would have to choose to do, against everything in your body that wants to stay exactly here.

choices.label: Get up, against everything, and take the cold stair down
Stay in the chair; let the stillness all the way in

blocks.s: You go up the floor toward the dais. The band sits in its half-circle, eight or nine of them in mess jackets, and they play without looking at their music, the same waltz, the held phrase, and not one of them looks tired, and not one of them looks at you, except the bandleader.
He stands at the front with his back half to the band, the baton loose in his hand, and he speaks before you reach him, flat, even, finishing the question you had not finished forming.

"...why we never finish it, no," he says. "You all want to know that. We bring it up to the last bar and we hold off the close. A waltz that resolves is a waltz that is over; the dancers bow and the floor clears and the night moves on to whatever is after. So we do not let it resolve. We hold the company on the last phrase, turning, and the night does not move on, and nothing after it ever has to come." The baton lifts, a half-beat ahead of the band, and the phrase begins again. "I have conducted this bar more times than there are dots in the part. Somewhere in it I mislaid the tune I first sat down to play."

choices.label: Ask him what he was first to play, before all the crossings
Ask him about the one who keeps this ship
Ask him to play the last bar, just once, and let it end
Leave the dais and cross to the long-aboard dancer

54.title: A Book You Do Not Turn (an ending)

blocks.s: You take a deep chair in the reading room and a book off the shelf, only to rest a moment, only until your legs are steady and the band is less loud in your head. The chair is the right give. The lamp is the right low green. The book is a good one, or it would be if you turned the page, and you mean to, in a moment.

The other passengers in their chairs do not turn their pages either. You notice this without alarm. You are all of you settled in the deep green quiet with the band a rumour through the bulkheads and the watertight door dogged at the end of the room, and there is no draught, and no morning, and no reason at all to get up.

There was a stair you meant to go down. There was a name you came aboard carrying. It is here somewhere, in your pocket, you will take it up presently, when you have read a little, except that you do not read, you only hold the book open at the one page in the warm green light, and the page does not turn, and neither, in the end, do you, and the watertight door stays dogged, and you stay in the chair, kept by a book you will never finish in a room where the band you came to leave plays on past every bulkhead, flat, and held, and never done.

The brass dog will not turn under your hand. It is not stiff; it is fixed, set into the metal, hard over against you. The way below the waterline is not open to a passenger the ship has not properly acknowledged for it.

You go back up the cold stair into the warm, to the foyer at the foot of the grand staircase, where a steward waits with the stamped card you carried aboard, the one with the coffee-ring and the real date, and holds it out to you. To go down, you must let the ship board you on, the way the purser boarded you on at the gangway. The watertight door wanted you crew before it would open; this door wants you a passenger written into the book. You take the card. The steward marks it. The ship acknowledges you for the way below, and the cold stair is open now.

choices.label: Go back down the cold stair to the door, boarded now

56.title: The Cup at the Foot of the Stairs

blocks.s: You take the cup. It is beef bouillon in a gimballed holder, the holder swung level on its ring though the ship is dead still, and the warmth of it goes up your arms and into the cold the deck left in you. The steward is glad. They are all glad here.
"You will have come down chilled, sir, madam," the steward says, certain of it. "There is no hurry at all. The night is young, and stays young. Drink it while it is hot."

It is good. The cold goes out of your hands, and with it goes a little of the edge you came down the stair with, the small insisting part of you that was keeping the count. You did not decide to stop counting. You only notice, a moment later, that you have.

choices.label: Go through to the ballroom with the cup warm in your hand
Set the cup down and go to the stopped clock
Let the steward sit you at a card table to finish it

blocks.s: You lift the carriage clock out from under its dome and close your hand around it. It is warm from no lamp, only from running, and it goes on ticking in your palm, fast and certain. You hold it to your chest a moment, and you can feel the seconds going through it, real seconds, the kind that pile up into minutes and hours and mornings.

The reading room cools by a degree it would deny. A passenger in a deep chair looks up from a book they are not reading and looks at you a beat too long before looking down. You carry a clock that runs now. You are a passenger who can tell the time, which is to say a passenger who can tell that the night is being done to them. The room likes you the less for it, and you find you do not much mind.
You put your thumb on the clock. As long as it ticks, you can count.

choices.label: Go to the man at the watertight door, watch in pocket
Go back to the foyer and through to the ballroom

blocks.s: You stand your ground at the clock and you say the year again, louder, all of it, the number and the street and the city off the brass plate, into the warm, daring the room to deny it. The room does not deny it. The room does worse than deny it.

The warm goes out of the foyer like a held breath let go, all at once, and it does not come back. The lamps burn on but they do not heat, and the carpet is a cold skin over cold steel, and the dancers in the rooms beyond have stopped, all of them, and turned, every blank kind face of them, toward the foyer and the clock and you. And up on the bridge the stillness with shoulders to it has fixed its whole cold attention on the one passenger who stood in a lit room and shouted the year, and the inner doors of the public rooms, the ballroom doors, the reading-room door, the doors down, swing to, soft, and the brass dogs turn, and seal.

You are right, is the terrible thing. You know exactly what year it is. You stand at the foot of the dead clock with the right year in your mouth and the whole warm machine of the night turned cold and shut against you, marked, awake, certain, and entirely sealed out of the warm rooms and the way down through them both, the one passenger who knew the truth and said it too loud to be let any further toward the door, kept now not in the warm but out of it, on the cold gilt skirting at the foot of the stopped clock, in the company of the standing water that does not climb to take you and does not fall to free you, for as long as twenty past two will last.

You stop. You let the stillness all the way in. Whichever warm thing has hold of you, the chair or the cards or the floor, you stop holding any part of yourself back from it, you let the count go, you let the edge go, you let the small insisting awake part of you that has been keeping watch since the gangway lie down at last and be still.

It is, for a moment, the purest relief you have felt in years, longer than years, since before the morning you ran. Nothing is being asked of you. Nothing more can be taken from you. The clock is stopped and you have stopped with it, and there is a peace in the stopping so total it frightens the last waking corner of you, which goes quiet too.

The stillness settles into you and finds it fits. You understand, dimly, from very far back, that this is the deepest stitch the night has put in you, deeper than the cup, deeper than the waltz, and that a passenger who has let the stillness all the way in is a passenger the ship has very nearly got. But it is so quiet. It is so still. It would be such a small thing now to let go the last of it.

choices.label: Let go the last of it; sink into the stopped peace for good
Find the waking corner; haul yourself up and take the cold stair
Your running watch ticks in your pocket; count yourself back awake

blocks.s: You read the year off the brass plate and you say it aloud. Not a whisper. You say it to the foyer, the number, the real one, the year it is or was or ought to be ashore where time runs forward into mornings.

The warm goes out of the room. Not slowly. The lamps do not dim but the heat leaves them, and the carpet underfoot is just carpet over cold steel, and the dancers out of sight stop turning, and a long way up, on the dark of the bridge you cannot see from here, a stillness with shoulders to it turns its attention down the long sweep of the ship and onto you. The bridge has heard a year said aloud. It marks the one who knows what year it is, the one who came up out of running time.

A steward is at your shoulder, no longer glad, almost frightened for you. "We do not say the year aboard," they say, very low. "You have marked yourself now. Go down quietly, if you have anywhere to be, before the cold up there has a proper look at you."
The year is in the air and cannot be unsaid. The room knows you for what you are.

choices.label: Go down toward the ballroom, marked, before the cold looks closer
Stand your ground at the clock and say it again, louder

61.title: The Girl Who Will Not Be Served

blocks.s: There is a girl against the wall by the doors, sat sideways on a gilt chair with her shoes off and her feet tucked under her, and a steward is bent over her with a tray and a cup, and she is waving it off with the flat of her hand, again, the way you wave off a thing that keeps coming back. She is in a first-voyage frock too good for her that she keeps tugging straight at the shoulder.

"No," she is saying to the steward, pleasant and immovable. "No cup. No chair by the band. No nice sit-down. I know you have to keep offering; I do not have to keep saying yes." The steward withdraws, glad as ever, and will be back. She looks up at you and reads you off in a glance, frank and tired. "You came down on purpose. The returning ones always have the look. Let me guess. You crossed the floor and they handed you your morning, all done up with a name on a card, and now you are wondering whether to dance it away."

She does not wait for an answer. "Do not dance it away. That is the whole game down here. They take the worst thing you ever carried and they make it warm and then they make it go, and they call that a kindness. It is not. It is just stopped."

choices.label: Ask her how she keeps from being warmed under like the rest
Leave her to it and go to the floor

blocks.s: You turn the brass dog and the watertight door swings in, and the cold comes through it whole, salt and old iron and the flat dead smell of water that stands and is never let move. Beyond the sill the stair goes down into the dark of the ship below the waterline, and at the foot of it, not far, the water lies across the deck, black and level, standing at its mark, giving back the last working light without a ripple.

The warm is behind you now, the band and the floor and the held bar, the dancer turning with someone else's morning, the child at the glass waiting for a water that will not climb. You have the card in your pocket. You have faced the shape of it, the morning you fled and the name it was going to take, and you have not yet paid for the facing, and you can feel that the paying is down here, in the cold, below the line where the warm can follow.

Behind you, a long way up the cold stair, a single pair of hands comes together once, slow, one clap, and stops, the sound a thing makes when it has weighed something and set the verdict aside for later.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm still, but thinner now, reaching down the cold stair after you:

Down again, and past the warm. You always could leave a room while the band still wanted you. Go on, then, down to the water. I will keep your chair, and your card, and the dance you did not dance; I keep everything down here, it is the one thing I am for. Mind the cold. I never could abide it myself, on the crossing I made; I never did get warm again, after.

blocks.s: The stair takes you down toward the standing water, and the long flooded alleyways, and the staterooms where the one in your coat is waiting with an offer, dry where you are wet.

You step through the watertight door and start down the dark companionway, away from the band, toward the cold and the standing water, because the way down is the way out and you want out. The Heavy dogs the door behind you, soft, and the warm is gone, and the stair goes down into a dark that smells of the sea coming in.

But you went down without the thing you came down for. You never crossed the ballroom floor. You never let the band, or the bandstand, or the long-aboard dancer bring you the morning you fled, the date, the face, the thing time was going to take. You only ran past it toward the dark, the way you ran once before, on the morning itself.

You stand on the cold stair with the water below you and the warm sealed off above, and you understand, in the dark, that you cannot leave by a door you reached by running. There is a thing waiting on the ballroom floor with your name on it, and the ship will not let you down past it unfaced. You climb back up. The Heavy undogs the door without a word and lets you back into the warm, and tips his chin at the ballroom, and goes back to his count.

choices.label: Go through to the ballroom and face what is waiting

blocks.s: You ask him about the one who poured the welcome over you off the bridge, the one that calls itself the Curator. The baton stops. He does not finish your thought this time; he gives one of his own, which is rare enough in him that you stand very still to hold it.

"...the Curator," he says. "Oldest hand aboard, older than the ship, the stokers say. Came down a crossing of its own, an age ago, running from a morning it could not walk into, and somewhere on that crossing it struck a bargain the rest of us never were offered, and the crossing it made does not land. So it keeps everyone else's night so beautifully, sees us all aboard and seats us in the warm and holds the waltz on the lip of its close, because it has no far shore of its own to make for and never will." The baton lifts. "It will be kind to you. The kindness is the whole of how she works, and it is enough. Do not stay to be kept."

choices.label: Ask him to play the last bar and let it end
Go down to the dancer and the floor

blocks.s: You ask him to play it. The last bar. The close. Just once, the resolving chord, and let the waltz end and the dancers bow and the night tip over at last into whatever is after. He looks at you, the baton low, and for a moment something in the flat face wants to.

"...let it end," he says, your words a half-beat before you. "I cannot. The stick goes up to it and the hand will not bring it down on the close. Not won't. Cannot. The same thing wrong with the Curator is wrong with the band; we are held one bar short of our own finish, all of us, and we cannot play ourselves past it." He lifts the baton to the lip of the last bar, and the whole room leans, the dancers slowing, the chord gathering, the end so near you could touch it, and the hand does not fall, and the phrase turns over, and the floor turns on, and the bar is unplayed again.
"You felt it nearly land," he says, flat. "They all do, the once. It does not land. Go down, if you want it to land. It does not land up here."

choices.label: Stay for the next turn; it almost ended, it might yet
Go down to the floor and the dancer; the floor is the hinge

blocks.s: You read the name. It is penciled in your own hand, soft and gone furry at the edges from a thumb that has rubbed at it, against the first dance and the second and on down the card, a name you know in your body before you can hold it in your head, a stair found in the dark by the give of it. It is the name of the person the morning was going to take. Or the person you were going to be, that the morning was going to take from them. From this far down the card you cannot yet tell which, and the not telling is the shape of the whole thing.
