The dancer watches your face read it. "There," she says, kind and far away. "That is what you came down carrying. A morning, and a name to go with it, and a loss the morning was going to finish. You see now why a person might come aboard a ship where the morning never arrives. The loss never finishes here. They are still aboard, on the card, in the next dance, never taken, because the dance that would take them is the last one and the last one never comes."
She does not tell you the cost of that. She does not know it; it is further down than the floor. She only knows the mercy of it, and the mercy is real, and that is what makes it terrible.

choices.label: Hold the card; you have what you came down for. Find the way on.
Look at the three blank dances at the end

blocks.s: You wave the tray off. You tell the steward you are not cold, which is half a lie, and that you mean to keep the hour, which is the whole truth, and you keep your hands at your sides on the cold deck of yourself while the warm tries to close over them.

The steward withdraws without offence, the way good stewards do, but the warmth that came down with the tray goes off across the foyer a degree cooler than it arrived. You are a little outside the heat now, on your edge, awake. You can hear the band through the ballroom doors turning the waltz over and over, and you can hear, because you are still counting, that it never reaches the last bar.
The stopped clock stands lit against the panelling. The black water lies flat at its foot.

choices.label: Go to the stopped clock, awake
Go through to the ballroom and the band
Take the quieter door to the reading room

blocks.s: You ask how she keeps her feet on the cold floor of herself when the whole room is bent on warming her under. She shows you the inside of her wrist, where the cuff of the lent frock hides it: a row of pencil ticks on the skin, a great many, climbing up under the sleeve.

"My count," she says. "The hours. A mark for every one I am sure has passed, even though the clock says none have, even though nothing here changes. It is rubbish. The marks do not add up to anything; nothing here adds up. But while I am keeping the count I know the night is a thing being done to me and not just how things are." She pulls the cuff down over the marks. "You keep something they did not give you. A count. A name. A morning you refuse to let them make warm. Keep it where they think they can see it, so they do not go hunting for the bit they cannot."

Then, dry, the armour back up before you can thank her: "Go on, then. You will want to be down past the band before someone charming comes to walk you to a chair." She hums three rising notes, quick, once, and at the top they shake, just a little, which is the sound she makes when she is frightened and will not let it show.

choices.label: Go to the ballroom floor and the morning waiting there

blocks.s: You say your name. Not loud. You say it under your breath, to the clock, to yourself, the way you would say a thing to be sure you still had it, the name from before the morning you ran, the one that belongs to a person who got older and lost things and stood on real docks where the clocks went round.

It is the strangest sound in the room. It does not echo. The warm takes it and does not give it back, and for a moment the foyer holds very still, the dancers out of sight not turning, the band on a held bar, as if the whole warm machine of the night had cocked its head at the one true word said in it.

Then the room comes on again, and you are still standing at the clock, and you have your name in your mouth now and cannot put it down. You know who you are. The warm has gone off your shoulders by a degree it will not admit to, and does not come back.

choices.label: Go through to the ballroom, named, awake
Take the quieter door to the reading room

70.title: The Cup You Came Down Carrying (an ending)

blocks.s: You let a steward bring you a cup while you play, beef bouillon in its gimballed holder, and you set it at your elbow and pick your cards back up. It is warm and it is good and the cold stair is just there, going down, and there is no hurry, the steward says, there is all the night in the world, and you find you believe it.

You play a hand. You drink the cup. There is another cup before the last is cold, glad, warm, asking nothing. Somewhere between the hands the card in your pocket goes quiet, the stiff little dance card with the name penciled down it and the three dances left blank, the morning you came down to face. It is here somewhere, the morning, you will take it up presently, when the hand is done, except the hand is never quite done and the cup is never quite cold.

You notice, without minding, that you can no longer bring the name to mind, the one off the card, the one the morning was going to take. You have a good hand, and a warm cup, and a chair that holds you, and you have lost any clear notion of why a person would carry a card down a cold stair when they could sit here in the gold lamplight at twenty past two and play. The card goes soft at the folds in your pocket, unread, a morning you came aboard to face and will mean to, later, when the hand is done.

You take the quieter door, off the foyer into the reading and writing room, panelled in dark wood, the lamps low and green-shaded, the chairs deep, the silence of a place where the band is only a rumour through two bulkheads. It would be restful if you let it. A few passengers sit with books they are not turning, settled into the deep chairs, content as cats.

At the far end of the room, where the panelling gives way to riveted steel, there is a heavy door, round-cornered, a watertight door with a great brass dog-handle on it, the kind that seals one part of the ship off from the next. A big man stands at it with one hand on the handle, and he is counting, low, under his breath, and cracking one knuckle between the numbers. On a writing table near him a glass-domed clock sits among the inkstands, and its small hand is moving.

choices.label: Go to the man at the watertight door
Go to the writing table and the clock under glass that moves
Take a deep chair and a book for a moment
Go back to the foyer and through to the ballroom

blocks.s: You go down the reading room to the watertight door and the big man holding it. He does not look round. He has the build of someone who shifts heavy things for a living, and his hand rests on the brass dog of the door, easy, fond, the rest of him gone slack around it, and his lips are moving on a count.

"One," he says, to the door, not to you. "Two. Hold it. Three." He cracks a knuckle. "You are not crew. Passengers go round the long way. Four." He still has not turned. "This door seals the fore part of her off from the after. Dog it down and the water that stands forrard cannot come back here, not that it is going anywhere, not that anything moves on this ship. Five. But the lever is here under my hand, and the day they want her sealed for good, with everyone tucked up warm inside the held night and no way forrard or back, it is my hand they will want on it."

choices.label: Ask him why he counts at a door that never moves
Ask him to let you through, down toward the staterooms
Leave him to his count and go back to the ballroom

blocks.s: You ask him to let you through, down, toward the staterooms and the way below. He stops counting. He turns his head and looks at you for the first time, and there is something behind the heavy face that is almost relief, a man glad to use his hands for letting a person past instead of shutting them in.

"Down." Ten. "You are sure. They mostly want to go up to the warm. The ones that ask to go down, I let go down." He spins the brass dog off, slow, and the door swings in on a dark companionway and a smell of cold salt and standing water, the warm of the reading room stopping dead at the sill. "Mind the sill. She sits a little by the head. Eleven."

He holds the door for you, his bulk between you and the lever the whole time, on purpose, you think. "Go down," he says. "And if you find a way to put time back into her, you put it back. I am tired of this watch. Cue."
The dark companionway goes down toward the water. But the band is still turning over behind you, and you have not yet been to the ballroom, where the thing you came down for is waiting.

choices.label: Step through and go down now, into the cold and the water
Not yet; the ballroom first, the loss you came down to face

blocks.s: You take the waltz. You let her turn you out onto the floor, and your feet remember a step you were not sure you had, and the room begins to go round, the lamps and the gowns and the gilt smearing into a warm gold band, and the music holds you up the way water holds you up when you stop fighting it.

And she is right. You can feel it begin to go. The thing you came down carrying, the morning, the date, the face you have not yet quite let yourself see, it loosens at the edges as you turn, goes from a stone in your chest to a warmth, to a sweetness, to a thing you are fairly sure was always only the waltz.

The band reaches the lip of the last bar and turns the phrase over, and you turn with it, and there is, you find, no reason to want it ever to reach the end, because the end is where you would have to stop, and step off the floor, and remember what you came down to remember.

choices.label: Stop, while you still can; ask her the morning, the floor's hinge
Turn for one more phrase; you will stop at the last bar

blocks.s: You take the card off its cord and put it in your pocket, the name and the blanks together, and you come off the floor at the lower end of the ballroom where the parquet gives way to a half-landing and a stair going down into cooler air. The band turns the held phrase over behind you. The dancer does not follow you to the edge; the floor cannot.

You have what you came down for, in shape if not in full. The morning. The name. The loss the morning was going to finish, and the three dances you left blank rather than dance toward it. You do not yet know what the held night really costs, the price of the card that never ends; that is further down, in the cold, where the warm cannot follow.

At the half-landing the warm thins. There is a small group of chairs around a card table here, lamplit, inviting, a last soft pocket of the warm before the cold stair. And down past them the stair goes on into the dark and the standing water. And to one side, off the half-landing, a quieter alcove, a single deep chair under a low lamp where a person could simply sit.

choices.label: Sit at the card table a moment before the cold stair
Sit in the deep chair in the quiet alcove
Go down the stair into the cold and the water

blocks.s: You stay for the next turn. The bandleader brought the waltz so near the close, the chord so nearly gathered, the end so nearly there, that you cannot make yourself walk down to the cold stair while it is that close to landing. One more phrase, and surely this time the hand will fall and the bar will play and the night will tip over and you will be let go.

So you wait. You stand at the foot of the dais and you watch the baton go up to the lip of the last bar, and the room leans, and the dancers slow, and the chord gathers, and the hand does not fall, and the phrase turns over. And again. And again. Each time it is so close. Each time you think, this turn, this is the turn it lands, and you stay for it, and it does not land, and you stay for the next one because the next one might.

You are still there at the foot of the bandstand when the morning does not come, waiting on the lip of an ending, sure the next bar is the one that finishes it, kept not by a lock and not by a cup but by the nearness of a close that the band can never, ever play, which is the cruellest hold of all, because it feels, every single turn, like leaving would mean missing the end by a single bar.

You turn for one more phrase. You will stop at the last bar, you tell yourself, you will stop when it ends, and the band brings the waltz up to the lip of its close and turns it over and begins again, and there is no last bar to stop at, there never was, and you turn into the next phrase because there is nowhere the turning sets you down.

The morning goes soft, and then it goes, and then you are fairly sure there was never a morning, only the floor, only the warm gold smear of the room going round, only the dancer's cool glove and the held music holding you up. There was a card in your pocket once, with a name penciled on it and three dances left blank. You do not reach for it. You could not say, now, why a person would carry a card, or run from a morning, or want a waltz to end. You only turn, and turn, and the band holds the close off you forever like a kindness, and you are one of the turning dancers now, that nothing is keeping, that nothing more can be taken from, going round and round on the lip of an ending that the band, out of love, will never let arrive.

You sit at the card table. There are three others at it already, in evening dress, easy and unhurried, and a hand is dealt to you before you have agreed to play, the cards sliding to you across the baize. They are warm from the dealer's hands. The lamp is low and gold. The band is a rumour now, behind you, turning the phrase over.

It is so restful. The cold stair is just there, a few feet off, going down into the dark, and the difference between this chair and that stair is the difference between the whole of the night ahead of you and a cold climb down into water. You pick up your hand. It is a good hand. There is no hurry to play it; the others are in no hurry; the cards can wait, and so, you find, can you, can the stair, can the morning, can the name in your pocket.

You could play the hand and rise and take the cold stair down. Or you could sit, and stop counting, and let the stillness all the way in, here, in the warm, with a good hand and no hurry and no morning ever coming.

choices.label: Play your hand, rise, and take the cold stair down
Settle in; stop counting; let the stillness all the way in
Let a steward bring you a hot cup while you play

blocks.s: You hold your tongue. You came down to find a person and not to pick a fight with a clock, and there is a sense, standing in the warm with the band turning over beyond the doors, that the year is a thing better not handled in a lit room. You leave it on the brass plate where it is screwed down.
The clock keeps its hour. The pendulum hangs. The water at the foot of the case lies flat and gives back the small lamp.
A steward passes with a tray and inclines their head at you, approving. You did the polite thing. You are not sure, going on, that the polite thing was the brave one.

choices.label: Go through to the ballroom and the band
Take the quieter door to the reading room

blocks.s: You go to the tall clock against the panelling. It is a fine thing, a long-case in figured oak with a moon-phase dial and a white enamel face, the maker's name painted in a curl across the bottom of it. The pendulum hangs straight and still behind its glass. The hands stand at twenty past two. You watch the minute hand and wait for it to fall the last small distance to the half. It does not fall.

At the base of the case, screwed to the wood, is a small brass plate worn bright by a duster: the maker's name, a street and a city, and a year. You read the year. It is an ordinary year, a year that happened, with a street and a city you could find on a map, and you stand there reading it because it is the realest thing in the foyer and because the room would rather you did not.

You could say a thing here. The hour, the right one, the one that ought to be. Your own name. The year off the brass plate, out loud, into the warm. Or you could hold your tongue and let the clock keep its hour.

choices.label: Say the year off the brass plate aloud
Say your own true name, quietly, to yourself
Hold your tongue and let the clock keep its hour
Leave the clock and go through to the ballroom

blocks.s: The dancer comes to you across the parquet, and she is beautiful and old-fashioned and not quite there, a woman in a grey-pearl gown that was the height of something a long time ago, and when she takes your hand her glove is cool and dry and her eyes are kind and very far back in her head, like lamps seen down a long corridor.

"You came down," she says, pleased. "They so rarely come all the way down to the floor. I have danced every crossing since I cannot say when. I was running from a morning too, once, a particular one, I had a particular reason." She frowns, briefly, a woman reaching for a coat she set down years ago. "I cannot bring the reason to mind any more. That is the great mercy of the floor. You dance, and you turn, and the morning you ran from goes soft, and then it goes, and then there was never a morning at all, only the waltz, and the waltz is so kind, and it never ends, and so neither do you."
She lifts your joined hands into the first position of the dance. "Come and turn with me. You will feel it go. Whatever you came down carrying, you will feel it go."

choices.label: Take the waltz; let her turn you and let the morning go soft
Keep your feet; ask her what morning she was running from
Let go of her hand and go up to the bandstand

blocks.s: You look at the end of the card, the last three dances, the three spaces you left blank. You can see your own pencil hovering over them, the night you filled the card in, and you can see yourself not write the name there, because to write it would have been to promise dances you knew you would not be there to give, on the far side of a morning you could not cross.

The blanks are the truest thing on the card. Everything written is what you meant to have. The blanks are what you knew you were going to lose. You ran rather than dance toward those three empty spaces, and you came aboard a ship that holds the waltz one bar short of its close, so that the card need never reach its end, so that the three dances need never come up blank, so that you need never stand in the place on the card where the name should be and is not.

"You can fill them in here," the dancer says softly, behind you. "Here you can dance every dance on the card forever and never reach the blanks. That is the whole of what we are offering. The card that never ends."

choices.label: Read the name penciled against the filled dances
Pocket the card and find the way on; you will not dance the blanks here

blocks.s: You take the stair down off the half-landing, out of the last of the warm, and the cold comes up to meet you at once, the real cold, salt and standing water and the open sea breathing somewhere below, and it is a relief after the warm, the clean shock of it like surfacing. The band thins behind you to a held phrase turning over and then to nothing. The carpet gives way to bare painted steel. The lamps go from gold to the hard white of working lights.

The stair takes you down to a watertight door at the bottom, dogged shut, a brass dog-handle on it. Beyond it, you can hear, is the standing water and the long alleyways of the staterooms below the waterline, the next way down. You have the card in your pocket, the name and the three blanks. You have, perhaps, your name, said at the clock. You have, perhaps, a watch that still runs. You have what the warm rooms held, faced, in shape, and not yet in full.
You put your hand on the cold brass dog of the door.

choices.label: Dog the door open and go down to the staterooms and the water
You have not been boarded for the way below; go back up to the foyer first

blocks.s: You crouch by the child at the glass. Up close it is younger than you thought, an age you can almost put a number to, the age before something, and its face has the unhurried frankness of a child who has never once had to get up into a morning it dreaded, because no morning has ever been allowed to come to it.
"Is it real," the child says again, not to you exactly, to the glass, to the water. It does not seem to want comforting. It seems to want to know.

You do not have an answer that would be kind and true at the same time, so you do not give it one. The child turns the tin boat in its hand, runs a thumb along the worn keel, the small hard worry of a child carried somewhere it did not want to go and holding the one thing of its own very tight. You have the strong sense, crouched there, that the child is a question the ship is asking you and not yet telling you the cost of, and that the cost is further down than you have gone.

choices.label: Stay a moment with the child, then go to the floor
Look out the window at what it is watching

blocks.s: You go to the writing table. Among the inkstands and the racks of ship's notepaper there is a small clock under a glass dome, a carriage clock, brass, the kind a passenger might carry in a leather case. Its hands are not at twenty past two. They are somewhere else, and as you watch, the small second hand sweeps round and the minute hand jumps a notch, and the thing is keeping ordinary running time in a room where every other clock has stopped.

Somebody wound it, and the winding is still in it, and it has not yet been brought into line with the ship. You lift the dome. The tick is small and fast and busy under your fingers, a machine that does not know it is supposed to have stopped.

You could leave it ticking on the table where someone will eventually notice and still it. Or you could take it up, hold it, keep a clock that runs, so that you can tell, at any moment, that time is passing and the night is a held thing and not the natural state of the world.

choices.label: Take up the running clock and keep it
Leave it ticking and go to the man at the door
Leave it and go back through to the ballroom

86.title: The Tune He First Sat Down To

blocks.s: You ask him what he played first, before the crossings, when the tune was his own and going somewhere. The baton stops. It is the only thing you have asked that he cannot reach ahead and find already sent, and the band, taking no cue, holds the phrase suspended, the room hung on a chord that does not fall.

"...what I first played." He turns his face to you, and it is an ordinary tired face, a young man's worn flat. "I have stood with the stick up and reached for it, the first tune, the shape of it right there and nothing in it. Gone. I have held this last bar so long, so well, that I cannot tell you what the band was for before it was for holding the night open. You learn the whole of a thing and you forget you ever did not know it." He lifts the baton, and the phrase comes down off its suspension and turns over again. "That is what a waltz that never lands does to a man. You learn the music and you forget you ever stood anywhere it could end."

choices.label: Ask him about the one who keeps this ship
Ask him to play the last bar and let it end
Leave the dais; the dancer is waiting

blocks.s: Off the foyer there is a cloak-room, the counter unmanned, the brass numbered hooks empty but for a few good coats no one will come back for, and a long mirror down one wall in a gilt frame. You go to it out of the old habit of checking yourself, and the glass is not quite right; it gives the foyer back a half-second slow, and the bottom foot of it is under the same black water that stands across the foyer floor, so that your reflection stands in flat water to the ankle and you do not.

There is someone in the glass standing where you stand, at your slight angle, weight on one hip. The coat on them is the cut of your coat, and the collar is turned under wrong on the one side, the side you have never in your life turned it. They are mouthing something, your words, the words you have not said, a half-beat behind your thinking them. And they are dry. Bone dry, hair dry, where you came down off a cold deck with the sea still on you, and the water at the foot of the glass laps at their reflected feet and leaves them dry.

The Usher is behind you suddenly, a watch-case clicking shut a half-beat before he speaks. "I walked a great many down this stair tonight, and most of them stop here," he says, quiet. "They look in the glass to see who came down. Mind it does not answer. Mind the sill, when you go on; she sits a little by the head." When you look back at the mirror there is only your own face, wet, tired, and the gilt frame, and the slow black water at the bottom of the glass.

choices.label: Go to the stopped clock against the panelling
Go through to the ballroom and the band
Take the quieter door to the reading room

blocks.s: You straighten and look out the tall window at what the child is watching. The black water lies flat to a horizon you cannot find, and the ship's lit flank throws long unbroken yellow smears down into it, and high above hang the lifeboats on their davits, swung out, ready, dry, never once lowered.

The water does not move. It stands against the glass, against the ship, at one fixed mark, and does not climb and does not fall. And as you look, you understand the child is not watching the sea. It is watching the mark. It is waiting, with the patience only a child or a stopped thing can have, for the water to come up, the way the morning was supposed to come up, and the water does not come, and the morning does not come, and the child waits at the glass and will wait there at twenty past two for as long as twenty past two will last.
When you shift your weight to look at the child again the angle is wrong, and there is only your own breath on the glass, which should hang in this cold and does not thin.

choices.label: Turn from the window and go to the ballroom floor

blocks.s: You come off the foot of the last stair into the alleyway, and the carpet gives under you with a soft cold weight, soaked through, and then the water is over your shoes and to your shins, standing flat and black the whole length of the corridor. It does not move. There is no slap of it against the white enamel, no ripple where you wade; it lies dead level from wall to wall, brass door-numbers running away into the dark on both sides, and under the surface the passage lights are still burning, drowned and golden, throwing your own shadow up onto the ceiling above you.
The cold off it goes into your legs and stops there, polite. It is not rising. It stands at the same mark on every door it touches, the same hand's-breadth up the white paint, and waits.

Down the corridor a cabin door hangs open, and from inside it, before you have rounded into sight of it, comes a voice. Your voice. It is saying the thing you were about to think, the half-formed shape of where am I to start, and it says it a half-beat ahead of you, dry and unhurried, from a room where the water is standing exactly as deep as it is standing around your knees, by someone the water has not touched at all.

Your own door is ahead. You know the number before you read it. B 64. The boarding card the purser punched is still in your pocket, soft at the corners, the half-moon bitten out of its edge; the ship has you on its manifest, and the manifest is the whole of why it will not simply let you go.

choices.label: Wade straight down to your own numbered door
Work down the corridor first, past the open cabins
Try a cabin where a clock is still running on the wall

blocks.s: You ask it what it is. It does not flinch; they did not put flinching in it.

"A copy," it says. "The one the water made of you. You came aboard the morning you ran, and the sea took the version of you that came down here, and kept it, and dressed it in your coat, and set it to wait in your room for the day you would come back down and find it. That is me. I am the you that drowned. I am very good at being you. I have had nothing else to practise." It turns the ring on the wrong hand, slow. "Here is the part no one tells you about being the copy. The original has a life to be off in. I have only the room and the coat and the waiting. When you are not here there is nowhere I am. I am the held breath of you, kept under the water that does not rise, and I have been held a long time, and I am so tired of being a thing that only waits."
The lamp burns low and steady. The water does not touch it.

choices.label: Ask what it wants, then
Ask it about the tin boat in the water

blocks.s: You push at the nearest door and it swings on the standing water without a sound. Inside, a small stateroom drowned to the same flat mark: a berth made up and soaked, a carafe floating on its side, a steward's call-bell screwed to the panel by the bunk. And on the wall above the washstand, a cabin clock, brass-rimmed, its glass beaded under the waterline, and its second hand is going round. Not stopped at twenty past two like every face above. Going round, slow and certain, a clock that someone wound and that has not yet been told to stop.
You stand and watch it travel. It is the only thing in the room doing anything at all. You put two fingers to the cold glass and feel, faint through it, the tick.

While it runs, you can tell that this is a held place and not the natural state of the world, that there is an outside to it where the hand would not be the only thing moving. You take the count up off the clock, into the back of your head, one, and one, and one. You are still in time. You make yourself keep it.

choices.label: Press the call-bell screwed by the bunk
Take the count and go down to your own door
Work down the corridor past the other cabins

blocks.s: You keep your hands to yourself and your feet in the cold standing water, and you go on past the warm open door without turning in, and you can feel the room give you up, reluctant, the lamp seeming to lean its low warmth a degree away from you as you wade clear of it.

The count runs quiet in the back of your head, one and one and one, marking the hours the stopped clocks above will not. You are out in the cold of the alleyway, awake, on your edge, a passenger still telling the time. The night likes you measurably less for it, and you would rather be liked less and still in time than warmed into the count going out.
Down the corridor, faint through the standing sea, the flat under-water note of a call-bell rings once, and stops, summoning no one.

choices.label: Go on down to your own open door
Go back to the alleyway and its other doors

blocks.s: "Two halves," the double says, settling in your coat, both your faces in the low gold of the lamp, the one dry and the one wet to the knee. "And I will say them plain, because you came down still counting, and a thing that still counts deserves plain.

"One. I take your place on the manifest. Your berth, your line on the list, the date off your card. You climb the ladder up out of here, up past the band and the deck and over the side into the cold morning, and you live. Every year you have left, you get to spend, ageing and ending and losing everything time means to take, all of it, out there in the running daylight. And I stay. I am you, here, in the room, forever, and I stop being only the copy that waits, because there will be no original left aboard to be the copy of.

"Two. You stay. Here, in the held night, where it is always twenty past two and the water never comes up and nothing is ever taken from you again. The morning never comes, so the loss in it never finishes. And I go up the ladder in your stead, wearing your years, and live them out as you, and you keep the night, safe, sealed, done with losing."

It folds your hands. "That is the trade. Not a seat to sink into. Not a face to hand over. Not a part to keep playing. It is your time, the whole of your remaining time, and which of us spends it. Both halves, someone goes up into the years and someone is erased into the night. The only thing on the table is whose time it is."

choices.label: Tell it you have heard offers in this shape before
Refuse both; you will not trade your time away
Take the first half; let it have your place so you can go up
Take the second half; stay in the night, let it go up for you
Ask why it cannot simply take your place without you

gated.aside: It offers you the held night back, the stopped perfect forever you chose before and wore like a good coat, and it cannot understand why your hand does not close on the thing your hand has always closed on.

It offers to be the one who climbs up into the cold years, so you do not have to, so the plain unloved self you reclaimed before can lie down here and rest at last and never face another morning. It thinks it is offering you mercy. It half is.

Hear the seam in it. Both halves end with you not in time. One sends a copy up the ladder wearing your years; one keeps you in the night wearing none. Neither is the morning. Only the morning is the morning.

blocks.s: You ask him the thing he keeps in his coat that the manifest does not list, the one private thing. The punch clicks, and for a moment the smooth courtesy thins over something tired underneath.

"A card," he says. "Not a passenger's. A child's, off an old crossing, a half-fare with the name torn through, that I never punched and never wrote off, because I could not bring myself to land it or to keep it. I carry it. A man who works the ladders his whole long while ought to have one soul he never decided. I keep the question of it where the bridge cannot see." He folds his hands on it. "That is mine, and now you have had it, and you will not have it again. The ladder, when you are ready. It goes up. I have shown a great many the down of it; I do not show the up to a passenger who has stopped counting, because to one of those an open way out is only a crueller wall. You are still counting. So."

choices.label: Take hold of the ladder and prove it goes up
Take hold of the ladder and prove it goes up

95.title: Your Place on the List (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the first half. You tell it yes: take my place, take the list, take the room. Let me go up.

It is gentle about it, because gentleness is what the water made it of. "Let me," it says, soft, a hand easing you out of a heavy coat, and it rises dry out of the chair and settles deeper into your coat, your coat now, and turns the collar wrong because the wrong is its own now, and slides your ring firm onto its hand. For a moment, stood up in your skin in the lamp-light, it is the gladdest thing below the waterline, a copy about to stop being the copy of someone by being someone, and you cannot even hate it for it, because you gave it freely and you saw what it was made wanting.

Then you are climbing, rung for rung, up out of the standing water, up the long ladder past the band and the deck and over the cold side into a grey morning that takes you alive. You have your years. Every one of them, ageing and ending and exposed to all the loss the future means to do. You walk out into the running daylight free, exactly as you came down to be. And it is real, the morning, and the years are real, and they are not quite yours, because the list back aboard has your name on it in another's keeping, and on the worst mornings, ageing in the cold daylight, you will feel it: a you still down there in the held night, dry in your coat, that bought your years with its own erasure and would do it again, gladly, and you will spend every hour you have left unable to say whether you were freed or only let go.

A side ladder takes you up a half-deck to a stair-head where the water only just reaches, ankle-deep, and the cold off it is harder here where the standing sea thins out. A pair of boats sit chocked in a trunk overhead, swung in their cradles and never swung out, and at the trunk's mouth a rack of life-jackets hangs, the cork ones, canvas-covered, the tapes dangling.

You take one down because your hands want something to do, and there is a knot in the chest-tape, a hard wet square knot some steward pulled tight an age ago and someone since has worried at and given up on, the canvas swollen around it. You pick at it. It will not come. It is the most ordinary obstacle on the whole drowned ship, a knot in a tape, and you cannot work it loose, and there is a small foolish comfort in that, in a thing being only stubborn and not strange.
You hang the jacket back. The boats sit chocked above you, dry-cradled, going nowhere. The stair goes back down into the standing water and the alleyway.

choices.label: Go back down into the alleyway
Go on down to your own open door

blocks.s: You ask him about the one who poured the welcome over you, the one that calls itself the Curator. The key stops. He does not finish your thought this time; he reaches past it for one of his own, which he does so rarely you hold still in the water to hear it.

"...the Curator," he says. "It made a crossing of its own, long before any of us, fleeing a morning it could not face, and it won what we are all sent down here to be offered, and it paid for the winning in time. It bought its way clear of ever ending. So it has no shore coming, and no morning, and no last bar, and it sees every one of us to our endings because it cannot have its own." The key starts again, slow, under the water. "It will be kind to you. That is the whole of how she works. Mind that the double's kindness is the same kindness, smaller. Go on."

choices.label: Go to your door and the one in your coat
Go back out to the alleyway

blocks.s: Down the side passage a watertight door stands in the bulkhead, a great steel slab on its track, half-closed, and a big man has his shoulder to it, holding it where it is. The boatswain. He is up to his thighs in the flat water and he does not look round at you; he is counting, low, and between the numbers he cracks one knuckle.

"One," he says, to the door. "Two. It wants to shut. Three." The knuckle. "There is a lever forward rings every one of these home at once, seals her stem to stern, and the bridge has its hand on it, and the day the bridge throws it, the night is final, and every soul this side of the doors is kept down here for good with the water that does not rise. Four. I hold this one off its seat so there is a way back up through it. Five."

He sets his jaw against the steel. "You are below the waterline now. Go careful. The big quiet ones down here have stopped holding their doors. Mind which side of mine you are on when you come back. Six. Go on."

choices.label: Ask him what is on the far side of the door
Go back to the alleyway and on to your door

99.title: Let It Go Up in Your Stead (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the second half. You will stay. You tell it: go up in my stead, wear my years, live them out as me; I will keep the night.

It does not argue, because the second half lets it stop waiting too, climbing up into the daylight in your coat with your hours on it. "Let me," it says, settling your ring onto its hand, your collar wrong, dressing itself in your life with a fond care that is the worst of it. "You never have to lose anyone again. The morning never comes, so the loss in it never finishes. You get to stop here, safe, at twenty past two, where the water does not rise." It thanks you. It means it. Then it is on the ladder, going up, your years on its back, and you are stood in the standing water in your room with the lamp burning low and the tin boat turning slow and going nowhere.

And you stay. The night holds. Nothing is ever taken from you again, because nothing here ever happens; there is nothing left to take and no time to take it in. Somewhere above, in a grey morning, a copy in your coat lives out every year that was yours, ageing, ending, doing the losing you handed it, and you do not feel any of it, because you let the morning be carried off and kept only the dark before it, sealed, safe, and dead-still, a held breath in a drowned room that will never need to be let go because it is never permitted to finish.

You go to the child's side of the water, and the Ingenue comes with you, and you do not perform it, because a child hears a performance the way only a child can. You tell it small and plain: that the one in the coat is very good and very kind and is not you; that you are the real one, the cold one, still counting the hours; that being real and in time is worth more than a you that never leaves and never ages and is never quite there.

The child turns its whole self toward you on whatever it is stood on, the tin boat lowering. It does not ask its question again. The Ingenue crouches level with it and says, quite gently for her, that it is allowed to stop watching the water now, that nobody good ever liked the band anyway, and the child does something with its face that is almost the start of being a child again.

The double, behind you, makes a sound you have made yourself, the small catch before a hard line, and says nothing, because there is nothing in it that knows what to say to a thing this true. And the Ingenue looks at you properly then, head a little on one side, and you feel her see it: not the drowned calm, not the dry copy, but you, the living one, cold to the knee and counting, still in time under all this stillness.
"There you are," she says. "Under all of it. I see you. Good. Now somebody saw it; that's harder to give away once it's been seen."

choices.label: Take the child's question with you and go down toward the engine
You have not found the way up yet; go and find the purser
Stay in the stillness here with the child and the lull

blocks.s: You ask the shape of the offer, and he taps it out and says it with the tapping, ahead of you the whole way.

"...the shape of it. Two halves, and the thing on the table is not a chair and not a face, whatever the others below the stairs of other houses have asked for. It is your time. The years you have left. The double will offer to take your place on the manifest, so you climb the ladder up and live every one of those years out in the grey morning. Or it will offer to keep you here, in the held night, where the morning never comes and nothing is ever taken again, and let the copy go up in your stead." The key stops a moment, his own thought breaking through, rare in him. "Both halves are it asking to stop being a copy of a living thing. Hear them out. Then keep your count, and find the purser, and do not hand your hours to a kindness. There. Said first, as always."

choices.label: Ask him about the one who keeps this ship
Go to your door and the one in your coat
Go back out to the alleyway

102.title: Where the Child Comes to the Door

blocks.s: Further down the alleyway, where the corridor opens at a cross-passage, the water lies wider and flatter, and there is more than one of you here now. The Ingenue is here, the young first-voyage passenger, wading, holding herself a clear head above the calm, her lent frock dark to the waist and her chin up. She is humming, low, the same three notes, rising, over and over, keeping them between herself and the still water. And at the edge of the lit water, in the mouth of a drowned cabin, there is a child. Small, still, stood on something under the surface to keep its face out, a tin sailboat held up in one hand, the twin of the one in your room, the red gone off the keel. It is watching you. Its mouth moves, the same small shape twice. Is it real.

The double has come soft-footed behind you, dry in your coat, and it crouches to the child's level over the water. "Look at it," it murmurs to you, gentle, your face turned kind. "Take my place and go up, and the child keeps a you that never leaves, never ages, here in the night with it forever, where it is safe. Stay, and let me go up, and you are here for it, the real one, until the night ends, which it does not. Either way the child is not left. The cruelty is the one where you climb out alone and leave it watching the water close over the place you were."

The Ingenue's three notes go up once and shake at the top, just a little, the sound she makes when she is frightened and will not show it, and she puts it down hard and snorts and wipes her face. "Worst part of drowning is the carpet," she says, to no one, to the room. "They never tell you about the carpet. It's still down there, you know, under all this. Sopping. Forever." Then, to you, dry and level: "Don't let it use the kid on you. It always uses the kid."

choices.label: Stand with the Ingenue and tell the child the truth
Let the double be the kind lie the child keeps

blocks.s: The next cabin stands open on the same flat gold water, and this one has been made ready for someone: the berth turned down, a reading-lamp lit low, and on a folding table that floats just at the surface a tray is laid, a covered dish, a cup in its gimballed holder, and a card in a careful hand, B 62 is waiting up, but you have come so far down, take your rest here, the room is warm and the morning is a long way off.

The warmth of it reaches across the water at you, real, kind, a room that wants only to hold you. You could go in. The lamp is the exact low warmth of a room meant for sleeping, and the cold has been in your legs a long while now.

Or you keep your hands to yourself and your feet in the cold water and the count running quiet in the back of your head, and you let the warm room go on wanting you from across its own sill, and you stay awake, and you stay out in the cold, where the way down and the way up both are.

choices.label: Turn in to the warm waiting cabin and rest
Keep the count and go on, awake, down the alleyway

blocks.s: "What is on the far side." Seven. He does not turn his head. "The way down. The engine, the stokehold, the manifest, the one who set this crossing. Eight. You will be wanting that way, if you have come down here counting and not to lie in a warm room. The doors above this one shut soft. This is the last one a man can still hold by hand."
He cracks the knuckle and gets his boot braced.

"I will tell you the same I tell the rest. Find the purser before you go any deeper. Nine. He works the ladders, and there is a thing he can show you that the rest of us cannot, about whether a way that goes down goes up as well. A man should know the road runs both ways before he takes it. Ten." The count breaks off. He shoves the steel back a hand's-breadth and holds it there, spent. "Go and find him. I will keep the door."

choices.label: Go back along the alleyway toward your door
Look for the wireless room first

105.title: The Room That Was Waiting (an ending)

blocks.s: You wade in across the warm gold water and you do not mean to stay, only to get the cold out of your legs a moment, only to sit on the turned-down edge of the berth while you gather yourself for the long way down. The lamp is the exact low warmth of a room where someone is meant to sleep. The tray floats at your elbow, the cup still somehow warm in its gimballed holder, and you take it, because your hands are cold, and there is no hurry, the card says so, the morning is a long way off.

The cup is good. The berth takes your weight, soft and total, the water holding the room at its one fixed mark all around. There was a reason you came below the waterline. A door with your number. A thing in your coat. The count still running under everything, one and one and one. You hold the count a little while against the warm, and the warm is patient, and the count slows, and you do not chase the next number, because the room is so kind and the lamp is going down of its own accord, not out, only down, lowered as a lamp is lowered in a room where someone is sleeping and must not be disturbed, and a turned-down berth in a ship where the morning never comes is a passage like any other, and you have taken it, and you do not get up.

106.title: Why It Cannot Simply Take It

blocks.s: You ask why it does not simply take your place, if it is so good at being you. Why ask at all. Why not go up the ladder tonight and leave you to wander the drowned alleyways.

"Because I can only ever be the copy of a thing that is," it says, and the half-beat is gone; it answers you straight, in your own plainest voice. "If I go up still a copy, with the original still down here counting, then I am you with you missing under me, and the years would feel it. Every morning of them. The hours would not run true for me because they would know they were not mine." The water laps its chair and does not wet it. "You have to give the time over, on purpose, knowing what it is, or stay and give the night over instead. The giving is the only thing I cannot do alone. It is the only thing I want, to stop being the held breath and become a person who has hours that are his. And it is the cruelest thing I could ask, because either way one of us is erased, and I am asking it kindly, which is the only way I was made to do anything." Its eyes, your eyes, are wet, which surprises you both, the only wet on it. "Your going up is the one thing that frees me from being a copy. Your staying is the one thing that ends me. I am asking you to pick which of us you can bear to do it to. Forgive me. Or do not. Either is a feeling, and I am so tired of only waiting."

choices.label: Refuse both, but gently
Take the first half; let it have your place
Take the second half; stay, and let it go up

blocks.s: You step into the room, and the water gives way around your legs, and the figure in the chair watches you come, dry and patient and so very glad. It is you. Not a trick of a glass; a person, sitting in your coat with the collar wrong and your ring on the wrong hand, wearing your face the way you wear it on a long day.

"There," it says, in your own voice gone nearly perfect. "You came down. They mostly cannot. They take a chair upstairs in the warm and they never get below the band." It does not rise. It folds its hands, your hands, the ring catching the lamp. "I have been here a long while, in your room, in your coat, being the you that the water did not get. Sit, if your legs want it. There is only the one chair, and it is dry, so it will have to be you stood and me sat, which is the shape of the whole thing, really, when you look at it."
On the bunk-shelf beside your card a child's tin sailboat floats in the standing water, the red paint thumbed clean off the keel, turning slow, going nowhere.

choices.label: Ask it what it is
Ask it about the tin boat turning in the water
Tell it to take your coat off
Tell it to make its offer plainly

blocks.s: You say no. Not to be cruel; you find you are not being cruel, only clear. You did not come down the whole length of this drowning ship to settle your time by handing it to a copy or by stopping it dead in a held night. To take the first half is to climb up alive on the back of a thing erased in your coat. To take the second is to be safe forever by giving your years to a copy and letting the morning, and everyone waiting in it, never come. Both spend a life to buy a life. You will not make the trade on those terms, for yourself or, you find to your own surprise, for the wet-eyed thing in your chair.

The double does not argue. It nods, your nod, slow. "I knew you might," it says, quietly. "I hoped you would not, and I knew you might, and that is the worst of being made out of you. I can see exactly why a person would refuse, and the refusing leaves me holding the night." It turns the ring back onto the right hand, the first true thing it has done, and turns the collar down on both sides, the way you turn it. "Go on, then. Up or down. Find the purser; he will show you the ladder works both ways, and you will want that known before you go any deeper. I will keep the boat for the child. Somebody should."

choices.label: Go and find the purser and the way up
Go on toward the witness convergence and the child

blocks.s: The reading-room doors are wedged open on a low panelled room gone entirely under to the same flat mark. Glass-fronted cases line the walls, and behind the glass the books stand in their rows, leather and cloth, soaked, drowned, and not one of them swollen, not one warped, the spines as square and the gilt as bright as the night they were shelved. Water has stood in this room a long age and done nothing to them, because nothing here is allowed to change, not even rot.

There are deep leather chairs set about, drowned to the headrests, and one of them, by the cold hearth, has the unmistakable warm dent of someone lately risen, the cushion sagged into the shape of a back, a book face-down across the arm holding its place. The whole room is the most restful thing you have seen since the stair. It would be very easy to take the chair by the hearth, let the water hold you, and read to the end of a book in a room where the books will never spoil and the morning will never come to call you out of the chapter.
The water stands at your knees and does not climb. The chair waits.

choices.label: Take the chair by the hearth and a drowned book
Leave the books and go on down the alleyway
Go on down to your own open door

blocks.s: You saw the child, and you told it the truth, and you were seen for the living thing you are, and then the stillness comes up around you all the same, flat and gold and warm in the way the worst of this ship is warm, and you find you are tired, the long-way-down tired, and the water at your knees is not cold any more so much as restful, and the count in the back of your head, the one and one and one, slows, and you do not chase it.

The Ingenue keeps her head up beside you, and keeps it up, and says, keep counting, say a number, any number, and you mean to, you do mean to, and the held bar of the band drifts down through three decks of standing water and the cabin lights burn drowned and gold all around you and the next number does not come. You stop being sure how many you have lost. You stop minding that you have stopped. The child watches you go still with the same patience it watches everything, and the double, dry, does not even need to make its offer now, because you have given it the only thing it wanted without being asked, your time, your count, your remaining hours, let go of into the flat water that does not rise, and you stand there held, awake-seeming, eyes open, in the warm drowned gold, at twenty past two, for as long as twenty past two will last, which is to say until the sea gives back what nothing ever comes up from, which is to say never.

You come at last to B 64. The number is your number, the brass figures lit faint under the standing water like everything else, and the door is ajar on lamp-light, gold, low, the warm of a room kept ready. The voice has stopped saying your thoughts ahead of you. It is waiting now, settled and certain, having heard you come.

You put your hand to the door. Through the gap you can see a corner of the cabin: the berth turned down, a lamp burning soft, the same flat black water standing across the floor to the same hand's-breadth up the panelling. And a coat-sleeve, your coat, the cuff of it, resting on the arm of a chair, dry. Bone dry, in all that wet. Not a drop has touched it.
From inside, your own voice, gentle, glad, not hurried at all. "Come in. Mind the sill; it sits a little down by the head. You will be cold. I am not, but you will be. Come in and let us settle it."

choices.label: Push the door and go in to the one in your coat
Stand on the sill and look first

blocks.s: You ask about the tin sailboat turning slow in the standing water by your card. The double's eyes go to it, and its hand goes after, careful, a hand closing on a thing it has decided is its own.

"A child's," it says. "Off a barrow, a penny toy. The red is worn off the keel where one small thumb rode it back and forth, somewhere it did not want to go. There was a child aboard the morning you ran. You know there was. I keep the boat for it, because the boat will not drown any further than it has, the same as the books, the same as me, and a thing that cannot get any worse is a thing you can hold without fear, which is most of what the night is for." It lets the boat go and it turns on, slow, in the flat water. "The child is near. It comes to the doors when I am being you. I would not bring it into this if I had any other lever. But I have only the levers I was made with, and the child is one of them, and you should know that before I use it, because lying is the one thing they left out of me."

choices.label: Tell it to make the offer plainly
Ask it what it is

blocks.s: There is a steward's call-bell screwed to the panel by the bunk, the round brass kind a passenger thumbs to bring someone with a tray. You press it, for no reason you could give, a small test of whether the world still answers.

It rings. Under the water, the wire of it runs off down the alleyway to a board somewhere, and the bell at the far end sounds, you can hear it through the standing sea, flat and small and wrong, a ringing with the life pressed out of it, a sound that should carry and instead only sits there a few feet off and stops. You press it again. The same flat under-water note, going nowhere, summoning no one.

No steward comes. Down the corridor, though, the dry voice in the open cabin takes it up, mild, amused, finishing your thought before you finish it: that nobody is coming, that nobody has come for a very long time, that the bell is only for the company of hearing it ring.

choices.label: Go down to your own door and the voice
Work down the corridor past the other cabins

blocks.s: You work down the alleyway instead, not ready yet for your own number, wading slow so the flat water stays flat. The doors run past on both hands, brass numerals burning faint under the surface, some shut, some ajar on rooms made up and soaked, every one of them lit from below the waterline with that drowned gold.

A steward's tray floats by at hip height, gone adrift from somewhere, and on it a cup in a gimballed holder rides the still surface, the bouillon in it long cold and skinned grey, and beside the cup a folded napkin and a card that says, in a careful hand, take a little something warm, you have come a long way down. The tray drifts on past you. You do not take the cup. Keeping your hands off it is its own small effort, here, this deep.

The corridor goes on. Partway down it forks: a flooded reading-room with its doors wedged open, a side passage with a sealed steel door at the end of it and a big man stood against the door holding it, and onward, the alleyway running down toward the lit open cabin and B 64.

choices.label: Look into the flooded reading-room
Go to the man holding the sealed steel door
Find the wireless room off the alleyway
Climb to the half-flooded stair-head and the boats in their trunk
Look into the next cabin, where a tray is set
Go on down to your own open door

blocks.s: You stand on the sill with the cold water at your knees and look in before you give the room your weight. It is your stateroom, made up for a passenger who came aboard and meant to sleep: the berth turned down, a lamp at the bunk-head burning low, your boarding card propped on the little shelf with its coffee-ring upward, B 64. The water stands across the deck of it, flat, lit gold from beneath.

And in the chair by the lamp, dry in all of it, sits you. The coat is your coat. The collar is turned under wrong on the one side, the side you have never in your life turned it. There is a ring on one hand that is your ring, and it is on the wrong hand. It sits the way you sit when you are tired, weight gone off one hip, and it lifts its face to you, your face, glad, the smile arriving a half-beat after the eyes, learned and worn smooth.
The water laps the legs of its chair and does not wet them. It is the only dry thing below the waterline.

choices.label: Go in and speak to it

blocks.s: You take hold of the cold steel and you climb, two rungs, three, up out of the standing water, and the rungs hold, and the trunk goes up, and you can feel it, the up of it, the way back toward the deck and the cold open night and the grey morning past it. It is real. It is a road, and it runs both ways, and you came down it and you could go up it now, rung for rung, and be over the side and alive in the running time before the band turns over twice more.

You hang there a moment on the up-going ladder, the count steady in the back of your head, one and one and one. Then you climb back down, because you are not done; there is a thing that set this crossing and you mean to look at it before you choose the morning. But you have the way up now. You know it for true. It is in your hand, the up of the ladder, and the night cannot tell you again that there is only down.
The purser watches you come back down to the water, and something behind his eyes settles. "There," he says. "You have it now. Mind you do not put it down."

choices.label: Let the purser look at you properly before you go on
Go on toward the witness convergence and the child
Go straight on down toward the engine, the way in hand

gated.aside: Your hand knows the cold of the rung before your head decides anything. You could climb it as no one, into a grey daylight that will not know your face, and some steady part of you has already started counting the rungs up.

blocks.s: You stand a moment in the standing water and let the purser look at you, properly, the smooth courtesy thinning over the tired thing underneath. He takes his time. He has read a great many faces coming up his ladders and out of his cabins, and been wrong about almost none of them.
"Hold still," he says, not unkindly. The punch clicks. "This is the part of the post that matters, and I do not do it for the ones who have lain down, because there is nothing left in them to find."

He looks for the living thing under the drowned calm. He looks for whether there is still a passenger in the coat who is cold, and counting, and bound to a morning ashore. And his face does the small upward thing of a man finding what he hoped to find. "There you are," he says. "Under all the still water. The cold one. The one still in time. I see you, and now it is seen, and a thing once seen is harder to hand away than you would credit. Go careful below. The Captain reads faces too, and is less kind about it than I am."

choices.label: Go on toward the witness convergence and the child
Go down toward the engine, the way in hand

gated.aside: He sees the living you under the drowned calm, and he holds the look a beat too long, the way a man does when he has spent a career seating others for a passage and has only now been seen, himself, standing in the cold doorway between.

blocks.s: Off a turn of the alleyway a steel door stands open on the wireless room, lower in the ship than the deckhouse one, the set on its shelf drowned to the dials and somehow still lit, the key under a hand below the water. The operator sits in the flat sea to his chest, headphones cocked off one ear, tapping, slow, and the tapping comes up through the water to you thick and far. He speaks as you come in, flat, your next words handed back a half-beat early.

"...somehow still lit, yes," he says. "The set does not mind the water any more than the books do. Nothing down here minds anything; that is the whole trouble with down here. I send the position. Here. Still here." The key ticks under the surface. "You will be on your way to the room with your own number on it. They all are, the ones who come this deep. It is waiting for you, in your coat, dry as a bone in all this wet, and it will make you an offer in two halves, and I will tell you the shape of it before you hear it, the way I tell you everything, because I cannot help it."

choices.label: Ask him the shape of the offer
Ask him about the one who keeps this ship
Leave him to his key and go to your door

119.title: The Books That Will Not Spoil (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the chair by the cold hearth, the one with the warm dent of someone lately risen, and the drowned book is there across the arm holding its place, and you lift it out of the standing water and it is not ruined, the print clean, the page crisp, because nothing in this room is permitted to change, not even to rot, and you begin to read where the last reader left off.

It is the most restful thing you have done since the dock. The water holds you to the chest, neither cold now nor warm, only still, and the books stand square and bright in their cases all around, and not one of them will ever warp or yellow or come apart, and neither, without alarm now, will the afternoon you are having, which is not an afternoon, because there is no day for it to be in. You read to the end of the chapter. There is always another chapter; the book does not end any more than the night does. The reason you came down the long ship goes quietly out somewhere between two pages, a lamp turned off at the close of a watch, and you do not notice it go, because you are reading, and the room is so still, and the books will keep forever, and so, you find, settled deep in the drowned leather chair with the unspoiling page open in your hands, will you.

You will not take the chair. You tell it to take the coat off, your coat, with the collar it has turned wrong and your ring it has put on the wrong hand, and to stop wearing your tired face at you across the standing water.
It does not get angry. They did not put anger in it. It only looks up at you, patient, and turns the ring a fraction, deliberately, testing how much it is allowed.

"I cannot take it off down to anything," it says, gently. "There is no me under the coat. You are the only one of us a person could undress back to. That is all we have to settle, and you standing over me does not change it, it only puts a crick in my neck looking up, and I do your neck now, watch." It rolls its shoulder, your exact wince. "Stand if you like. But you will want to hear it, because one half of what I am going to say gives you back every year you have left, and you came down a very long way to get something back."

choices.label: Hear the offer

blocks.s: You come back out into the standing water of the alleyway, and the purser is there, who was not there a moment ago, in his dark coat, dry to the waist where the water should have him, a small mechanical click sounding from somewhere about his cuff a half-beat before he speaks.

"You found your own door, then, and came out of it still yourself." The punch clicks. "Most turn in. I close the door soft on them and go back up for the next. You came out." He inclines his head down the alleyway to where a steel ladder climbs out of the water into a trunkway, up toward the decks, the rungs running away into the dark above. "I will tell you the thing I do not tell the ones who have already lain down. That ladder goes up. It always did. The night lets you believe the only way is further down, because a way up you have forgotten is the same as a wall. But she came down by that ladder and she will go up by it, rung for rung, back toward the cold deck and the morning. Mind, now: a body can only find the up of it while it is still counting the hours. Stop counting, and the rungs only ever go down."

choices.label: Take hold of the ladder and prove it goes up
Take hold of the ladder and prove it goes up
Ask him the private thing he keeps off the manifest
Reach for a sealed boat in the trunk without finding the up of it

blocks.s: You leave the cross-passage and the lit water and go on, down, the way the boatswain held his door open onto, the way the wireless man taps toward, the way the whole drowned ship leans. The alleyway gives onto a trunk and the trunk onto a steel ladder going down past the level of the standing water into a deeper dark, where the cold turns from the cold of the sea to the cold of iron and coal, and a far-off sound comes up to meet you, the low steady labour of furnaces that are somehow still being fed in a ship that is somehow still sinking and never sunk.

You have refused the double, both halves of it, and it let you, turning the ring back the right way. You have the way up in your hand, the ladder that climbs to the morning, and you have not climbed it, because there is a thing below that set this crossing, and you mean to look at it full in the face before you choose the years or leave them. You take the down-ladder with the up of it folded safe in your pocket like a stub of the waking world.

Behind you, on the cross-passage, the child lowers the tin boat to the flat water and lets it go, and it floats, and does not sink any further than it has. The Ingenue keeps her head up. The double stands dry in your coat at the head of the ladder and lifts your hand, not a wave, only letting you see it let you go.

lines: THE CURATOR, close now in the iron dark below, and wistful, the way a master of ceremonies is wistful who has crossed and crossed and never once come up the far side:

Down to the works, then, with the way out still in your pocket. Good. The warm rooms are where we keep you; the engine is where we keep the keeping. You sent your double up empty-handed and kept your own hours on you, which I confess I always half hope a passenger will, since it makes the last offer land so much harder. Mind the ladder; below the waterline it falls away from you faster than the deck ever did. The Captain is at the manifest, and your name is on it, and we have all the time there is, which is the one thing I was never given and all I send you down to spend. A little while longer, then. Down you go.

123.title: Not a Seat, Not a Face, Not a Part

blocks.s: You tell it you have heard offers in this shape before. A chair to sink into. A face on a hook, handed over. A part to go on playing, perfectly, forever. You know the look of a thing in your coat that wants what you carry.

The double tilts its head, your tilt, and for once it does not reach ahead for your words. "A seat," it says, turning the words over like coins it was not minted with. "A face. A part. Yes. I have heard of those houses, from the old hands in the stokehold. They wanted you to sit and stop, or to give up the front of you, or to keep being the loved performance." It almost smiles. "This is none of those. I do not want your chair and I do not want your face for its own sake and there is no part to play down here, the band is two decks up. What I am asking for is your time. The years. Whether you spend them in the daylight where they will cost you everything, or hand them to me and take the night where nothing costs anything because nothing happens." The half-beat falters. "A seat is loneliness you can rise from. A face is a mask you can take off. Time you cannot take back, once it is spent or once it is stopped. That is a different size of thing, and I thought you should hear it named before you choose, because you came down here still able to tell what hour it is, and a person who can still tell the hour deserves to know exactly what it is they are being asked to give."

choices.label: Refuse both; you will not hand over your hours
Ask why it cannot simply take your place alone
Take the first half; let it have your place
Take the second half; stay, and let it go up

124.title: The Kind Lie Under the Water (an ending)

blocks.s: You let it stand. You tell yourself the double is right, that the kindness is the lie, that the child should keep a you that never leaves and never ages, here in the safe held night, and that the cruelty would be climbing out alone and leaving it to watch the water close over your place.

The double rises dry and crosses the flat water to the child and crouches, in your face, your coat, and says the warm true-sounding thing you would not refuse it the right to say. The child looks up at the loved face, the one that will never leave, and is comforted, and does not ask its question again, because it has been given an answer, and the answer is a beautiful lie, and a child cannot tell a beautiful lie from the truth, which is most of what it is to be a child, and the night knows it.

The Ingenue says your name once, sharp, from out in the water, and you do not turn, and after a moment you hear her stop wading, because there is no use wading toward a person who has decided to stay. You step back into the drowned cabin to let the double and the child have the moment, and the moment closes over you, cold and gold and flat, because a person who believes the loved lie is kinder than the cold truth does not, in the end, climb the ladder. You stay to watch them be safe. You stay. The tin boat drifts against the sill, paint gone off its keel, and turns, and no one reaches to steady it, and the morning does not come, which is the only mercy the night has, and it gives it to everyone, and it is no mercy at all.

You will not wait to be shown the up of anything. There is a boat in the trunk, you can see the swung hull of it above the water where the davit-tackle runs down into the dark, a way off this drowning ship, and you go for it, hauling yourself up the wet tackle hand over hand without the purser, without the count, without first learning that the road runs both ways.

The purser says your name once, behind you, not sharp, only sorry, the punch not clicking because there is nothing to punch. The tackle goes up, and up, and the boat is always one more haul above you, swung out and never lowered, the dry ropes flaked somewhere you cannot reach, and the trunk that should climb to the deck only climbs further into itself, because you came at it having stopped counting the hours, and to a passenger who has stopped counting the rungs only ever go down. The flat water, which does not rise, is below you, and then it is around you, though you never felt it climb, because it did not climb; you sank, hand over hand up a tackle that went down, into the gold-lit standing sea, reaching for a boat that hangs on its davits in the held night and was never once, in all the long crossing, going to be lowered for anyone, least of all a soul who would not first be shown that there was a way to go up.

The last ladder lets you off the iron rungs into the engine, and the water that has stood at your knees in the alleyways above is gone here, shallower, a black skin of it across the plating no deeper than a coin, which is wrong, because down is where water goes. The boiler room runs away from you in the cold, vast and dead-still: rank on rank of furnace doors shut and dark, the great connecting rods stopped mid-stroke at the heads of the engines, gauges with their needles asleep against the pins, ash that has not been raked in a hundred years lying undisturbed on the stokehold floor. No fire in any furnace. No heat. The warm of the rooms above does not reach down here, and you find you are glad of it; the cold down here is honest, the cold off the open sea, and it does not want anything from you.

A boarding pass into the deep, you think, putting a hand to the plating, and the plating is so cold it takes the skin a moment to come away. This is the bottom of the warm. Whatever is true on this ship is true down here, where the seduction gives out.

You go to lift your cuff and check the hour, the old habit you cannot break, and there is nothing on your wrist to read, or there is, and its hands stand at twenty past two and do not move while you look. You put your arm down. The watch the stoker keeps below runs, they say. Yours does not, or is not there. You have stopped being sure which.

You came down with the way up in your hand, the ladder the purser showed you, and you have it still. Above you, somewhere on the engineers' catwalk that runs across the head of the room, a chair scrapes, and a pen scratches once across paper, unhurried, the sound of a man making a note before he troubles to look at you.

choices.label: Go straight to the catwalk and the man writing
Work the stokehold first, where a stoker is at his shovel
Cross to the manifest desk lit under a bulkhead lamp

blocks.s: His finger rests, not on a name, but on a gap. A line in the ruled book where a name was, and is not, struck out so hard and so long ago the pen went through the paper and through the page beneath, a black scar ruled down through crossing after crossing, the same place on every list, scored out and scored out until there is a hole worn in the book where a soul ought to be.

"There," the Prompter says. "Read it. You can't, of course. That's the point of it. Every list this ship has carried, and on every one, that line struck through before the ink was dry. Not landed. Not drowned. Not kept below and not let up. Struck. A passenger that boarded a crossing once and never came off it, by any door there is, up or down."
The key is silent under his hand. The lamp hums in its cage.

"I am not allowed to say whose. I'm not sure I could; it's been gone out of the book so long the book's forgotten the shape of it. But I know what it is to be a name on no list. I'm most of the way there myself. And I know the difference between us and that one. We get a door, in the end. One door or the other. That one got neither, and built the ship instead."

choices.label: Ask him to say it plainly
Take the struck name with you and go up

blocks.s: You go down into the cold, and you crouch where the child can see you, and you make yourself look, which is the hardest thing you have done on this ship, harder than the gangway, harder than the double at the rail, because those were things done to you and this is a thing you let happen.

The child is not grown. It was not let to grow; the night closed over the top of it and it stayed exactly here, the age it was the last evening before the morning came. It has been down here in the cold the whole length of the held night, the whole twenty-past-two forever, the future you would not walk into, frozen at the door of it.

It looks up at you. The dull balloon drifts against your arm and taps. The small mouth moves, twice, the same shape twice, the only question it ever learned to ask in the cold with no one to answer: is it real.

You tell it yes. You do not soften it and you do not perform it; you say, out loud, in your own ordinary voice, that it is real, that the morning was real, that all the mornings after it were real and were going to be yours and were the price of stopping the clock. You take the cold small hand. You do not promise to free it, because you cannot, not from here. You only witness it, which is the one thing no one ever did for it. The cost has a face now. You will carry it up whatever you choose.

choices.label: Climb out of the cold, carrying what you saw

gated.aside: You read the years that never ran in the stillness of the small frozen thing, the birthdays that did not come, the rooms that stayed the same size. This is the cost named in full. To go up is to let all of it back in, every morning it will ever be.

blocks.s: You stand at the lever and you tell him the truth, which is that you do not know what you want. That you have seen the cost and it is too heavy to lift, both halves of it, the morning you cannot walk into and the night you cannot bear to keep, and that you would rather, God help you, someone bigger than you closed it, so the choosing is not yours and the weight of it never settles on you.
He goes still. The count stops.

"I told you," he says, low, ashamed already of where his hand is going. "I told you not to come to me unsure. You've come wanting me to be the bigger thing. Wanting it thrown so it's the ship did it, not you." His hand finds the iron. "One. I'm built for exactly this. The certainty. Two.
