And I'll tell myself it was a mercy, that you couldn't have borne the cold water and the long climb into the years, and I'll be lying, and we'll both know it. Hold." His eyes are wet and his jaw is set. "Last chance. Take your own self down that ladder and choose like a person in time. Or stand there."

You stand there. You let him. There is a great and terrible relief in it, the relief of a thing you will never have to own. "Three," he says, and his voice breaks on it, and he throws the seal. The door drops in the dark below, the moving water shuts away, and the held night closes over you smooth as poured plaster, fixing you in it, the choice made, settled from outside, never yours, never to be yours, the one mercy the ship was always most glad to grant.

You ask him, plain, to keep his hand off the seal. To let you go down past him to the breach on your own two feet, still your own, still in time, to make the choice yourself in the water rather than have it made for you here by a man and a lever.
He is quiet, counting something that is not a number now.

"Ninety," he says at last, and the knuckle cracks, and he takes his hand off the iron, slow, a thing wanted a long time and set down anyway. "I can hold off. It's not the holding that's hard. It's that the lever wants throwing the way a cold man wants a fire, and I'm the one stood next to it, built for exactly the certainty it's offering." He looks at you, and there is wet in the big flat face, and his jaw is set against it. "You go down. You go down knowing what you saw, and you choose it in the water, and it's yours. I'll keep my count and keep my hand still."
He steps back from the gate, one heavy pace, and the way down opens past him, a last ladder into the dark and the cold and the sound of water that is, at last, moving.

"One thing," he says. "Don't come back up past me unsure. If you come back wanting it settled by someone bigger so it's not your choice, I'll throw it, and tell myself it was a mercy, and we'll both know I lied. So don't. Go down sure, or go down unsure, but go down. Hundred. Clear."

choices.label: Go down the last ladder to the breach

blocks.s: You ask about the counting you keep half-hearing under the engine noise that is not there, low, a number and then a number, somewhere aft in the dark.

"That's the bosun," the stoker says. "At the lever. There's a watertight door betwixt here and the breach below, and a lever throws it, and when it's thrown she's sealed for good, every soul this side of it kept in the night with the engines. He stands at it and he counts. Always has. Counting's how he keeps his hand off it." A dry sound that might once have been a laugh. "Big quiet man, the bosun. The ship found the part of him that likes a door shut hard and built him a whole watch out of it. He hates it. He's very good at it. Those generally go together."
He looks at his mug welded to the deck.

"You want to keep your own count, down here. Same as he does, only for yourself. The cold takes the hours off you if you let it. Keep counting and you stay awake to the fact the night's a thing being done to you, and not just how it is." He grates the shovel an inch. "Keep it, and mind how you go. He's deciding the same thing you are, only he's got the lever and you've not."

choices.label: Take the stoker's own ladder down, your own way to the water
Go to the manifest desk and the log
Go up to the catwalk and the Captain

blocks.s: You ask him, plainly, what the held night cost. He is quiet a moment, the only quiet you have had from him, and when he answers the dryness has thinned.

"You want the cost named. Words will not do it; words are my trade and even I cannot make this one sit in a sentence. I will have to show you." He rises, stiff, and takes up the caged lamp by its handle. "There is a place aft of the boilers, the coldest in the ship, where the held night keeps what it took to be held. No passenger is brought there. The ones who ask to see it are the only verdicts I have ever been unsure of."

The count comes up then from somewhere aft in the dark, low, a number and a number, the bosun at his lever. And under it, fainter, three notes, rising, a girl humming, and at the top of the three the notes shake, just a little, the sound a person makes when she is afraid and will not let it show. The Captain hears it too. Something crosses the dry old face, gone before you can name it.

"That one is still counting her own hours," he says. "The newest aboard. She has not lost the count yet. It is a near thing whether she keeps it." He lifts the lamp toward the aft dark. "Come. You should see what you are weighing before you weigh it. Mind the sill. She sits a little down by the head, even here."

choices.label: Follow the lamp aft to the cold place
Go to the humming first, to the girl at the doors

blocks.s: You ask what he is, under the master's coat, under the post. It does not offend him. It comes the nearest he comes, the whole hour, to something almost kind.

"Under the coat." He sets the pen down. "There is no under, with me, and that is the difference between us. You came down this ship looking for the person you stopped being, the one beneath the part you ran from. I have not had a person under mine in longer than the manifest runs. I am the verdict. I am the sea's judgement of what is worth keeping, given a coat and a chair and a steady hand, and a thing like that cannot keep a self; a self has wants, and a judgement with wants is only a grudge in braid."
For a moment the dry eyes hold something that is not dryness.

"I was made out of a larger hunger that could not bear to do its own deciding any longer and needed a colder hand to hold the pen. I am a fragment of that appetite, fitted to a bridge. I do not say this to frighten you. I say it so you will understand that when I name your worth, it is not I who wants you kept. It is the thing I was cut from. I am only the part of it that learned to be fair."

choices.label: Ask who set this crossing

blocks.s: You went down into the cold and you looked at the child frozen at the door of the morning, and you took the small hand, and you climbed back up knowing the whole of it, the way you know a grief by having had it and not by being told. And knowing it, clear-eyed, with the cost named and no part of you fooled, you find you cannot do it. You cannot let the ship finish and surface alone into all of it, the grey morning and every morning after, the long slow losing by inches of everyone you held safe down here, and yourself last of all.

You tell the bosun to leave the lever be. Not to seal you in, and not to make you choose, only to let you be where you are. You go back, past the dead boilers, to the cold compartment, and you sit down on the cold chain beside the child that never woke, and you take its hand, and you stay.

It is not a defeat, exactly, and it is not peace, exactly. You have done a clear thing with your eyes open: chosen the held night because you have seen the morning whole and judged you cannot walk into all of it at once. The child does not wake; you did not promise it would. The balloon drifts low and taps the iron, dull, once. Above, the band turns over the same waltz it never finishes, and the count goes on at the lever, and you sit in the engine's cold quiet with the one frozen thing you would not leave alone in the dark, kept, witnessing, in time no longer, where nothing more will ever be taken because you have sat down at last in the place where nothing moves.

You have seen the cost, the whole of it, and you choose the night, and you do not want to carry the choosing. You tell the bosun to throw the lever. To seal you in, here, below, with the engines and the held breath and everyone you came aboard with, where the morning cannot reach and nothing more can ever be taken, and to make it final so that no later hour can talk you out of it.

He goes still, even the count stopping, which it never does. "You're sure," he says, flat. "You've seen it, and you're sure, and you want my hand to do it so it's done." You tell him yes. His jaw works. "Ninety. That's a verdict a man can respect, near enough. It's not the cowards ask me for the seal. It's the ones who've looked." The knuckle cracks. "Hundred."

He throws it. The lever goes over hard and the great door drops somewhere below in the dark with a long descending rush and a flat iron finality that is the most final sound you have heard on this ship, and the moving water you could just begin to hear is shut away under it for good, the breach sealed off, the choice it would have asked you sealed off with it. The night closes over the lot of you, smooth, cold, complete. The bosun turns his back, counting, one, two, the count he uses to stay standing through a thing he hates that suits him. Up in the warm the band turns over. Down in the cold the child sits unfreed at the door of the morning that will now never come, and you are sealed in with it forever, having looked, having chosen, having handed the last inch of the choosing to a larger hand so the door, and not you, would be the thing that shut.

You follow the three rising notes aft and down a half-deck to a watertight doorway in a bulkhead, a heavy steel door on rollers that is meant to be slammed shut and dogged in an emergency, and it is closing, slow, an inch at a time, the way a thing closes that a lever far off is starting to throw. The girl is in the gap of it, the newest aboard, in the first-voyage frock too good for her, and she has her shoulder to the closing edge and her heels on the wet plating, holding it, and she is humming, three notes, rising, shaking at the top.

"Oh, marvellous," she says, through her teeth, not stopping pushing. "You. Don't suppose you came down with anything useful this time either. They're closing her on me. Funny thing, they only ever start closing the door once you've nearly worked out how to leave by it." The door grinds in another inch and she swears, cheerful, terrified. "I kept my count, you know. The whole way down. Penciled where the ship couldn't see. I just need to get the other side of this rotten door before someone throws the big lever and I'm sealed in for the duration, which is forever, which is a long booking."

choices.label: Get your shoulder to the door beside her and hold it
You have no way to help; the door is too heavy

blocks.s: You cannot. You stand at the edge of the cold compartment with the lamp throwing its one reach of yellow into it and the small still shape down there on the cold chain, and the Captain waiting for you to go down and look, and you find there is a limit to what you came down here able to bear, and you have reached it one cold room short.

"I would rather not," you say, and your voice does the old thing, finds the steady weight and sets it down just so, calm rising in you easy and warm because the calm is the very thing that exists so you never have to feel the rest. "I know it's down there. Let that be enough. I don't need to climb into the cold and take its hand to know the cost."

The Captain looks at you a long moment, and something in the dry old face settles, not unkindly, into a verdict he has written many times. "As you wish," he says. "Most cannot. It is not weakness; it is the calm, doing the one thing the ship cast it to do." He lifts the lamp away from the compartment, and the dark closes over the small still shape, and you are spared the sight of it, which is what you asked for, and what it costs.

You go down to the breach able to choose anything and unable to feel the one thing under it, the cost a shape you were told and not a weight you carried, and whatever you decide in the water you will decide it as the one who would not look, and the child stays frozen at the door of the morning, unseen, unwitnessed, asking the cold its one question of a dark that you, of all the souls aboard, decided not to answer.

Against the after bulkhead a desk is bolted to the plating under a caged lamp, an engineers' desk turned to other work, and on it the manifest lies open: a great ruled book, column on column of names and berths and ports, a hundred years of crossings in the same exact hand. Beside it the ship's log stands open to a page, and the page is the wrong page, the only page that matters, dated in a real hand to a real morning, the ink black and unrun though the paper is wet through and cold to the touch. You read the date. Your body knows it before your eye finishes it, the morning off your boarding card, the morning you ran.

A wireless set sits at the desk's end, brass and bakelite, and a man is at it with the headphones cocked off one ear, the old long-aboard hand, the one whose call-sign stopped being his own crossings ago. He does not turn. The key is still under his hand, but it is not moving. He is looking at the manifest the way a man looks at a thing he has decided to do that will cost him.

"...the morning you ran," he says, flat, finishing the thought off your face before you have it. "Yes. It's all written down. He writes everything down. Sit, if you like. I'm going to spend something on you in a moment, and I'd as soon you were near when I do."

choices.label: Read the manifest where his finger rests
Ask what he means to spend
Leave him and go up to the catwalk

blocks.s: You go the wrong way down. Not the catwalk, not the manifest, not the lever and the held door, but back through the stokehold toward a ladder you half-remember, meaning to find your own way to the water and skip the verdict and the cost and the bosun and all of it, to keep the choice clean and yours and untold.

The stokehold is not as you left it. The black skin of water on the plating is rising, which it has not done anywhere on this ship, and the cold is coming up with it, and the dead furnaces stand open-mouthed and dark to either side as you wade, and the ladder you remembered is under water now, the rungs going down into a flooded well with no top to it and no bottom found. You went without the verdict, without the witnessing, without the held door, trying to reach the breach by a path the ship does not keep open, and the ship, which keeps every honest path open and every dishonest one drowned, simply lets the water have you.

The stoker is at his place still, leaning on his cold shovel, not looking up as the water takes the deck. "Told you not to set things down," he says, to no one, to the rising cold. "Down here you keep ahold of what you've got, or the water has it, and then it has you." The welded mug goes under. The lamp gutters. You did not reach the choice. You reached only the cold flooded dark of a man who would not go the way the ship laid honest, and it closes over you with no verdict spoken and no morning weighed, which is the one ending the Captain never has to write, because the sea writes it for him.
You climb back up out of the cold compartment, and you are not the same coming up as you went down, and the Captain sees it, and marks it, and does not gloat. He sets the lamp on the rail.

"Now you know what you are weighing," he says, and the dryness has gone thin enough to see through. "That is the cost named at last, and not in a sentence, because I told you it could not be done in one. To stay aboard is to keep that child frozen, perfect, never woken, and to keep beside it everyone you came aboard with, held, never gone, never taken, safe in the one night that does not finish. That is real. It is not nothing. A great many choose it, and they are not fools, and they are not cowards."
He turns the lamp toward the after dark, where the count goes on, low, a number and a number.

"And to leave is to let the ship finish her sinking and surface alone into the grey morning and every morning after it, all the years at once, where you will age and end and lose by inches everything time means to take, including the ones you held safe here, including yourself. That is real too. I will not tell you which is the braver. The post is not to tell you. The post is only to make sure you go down to the breach knowing both. The way down is past the bosun and his lever. Mind him. He is weighing the same thing you are, and his hand is on the seal."

choices.label: Go to the lever and the bosun, the way down
Go first to the girl at the closing doors
You have seen it whole, and you cannot leave; sit down in the cold and stay

blocks.s: You go up the short ladder to the engineers' catwalk that runs across the head of the engine room, a steel walk railed in pipe, lit by a single lamp, and the man is there at a small folding desk with the manifest's twin open under his hands, a master's logbook, and a pen. He is in a master's coat, the gold gone green at the cuff, and he is older than the ship, dry-skinned, dry-eyed, the look of a man who has read every soul that ever came down this ladder and been surprised by almost none.

He does not look up at once. He finishes a line in the log, and before he sets the verdict-word at the end of it his hands come together, once, a single slow clap that is not applause, the sound a man makes to mark that he has seen a thing and means to weigh it. Then the pen scratches the word, and only then does he look at you.

"You came down," he says, dry, exact. "They mostly do; down is the easy way, that is the trick of the whole ship. I am the Captain. I keep the manifest and I say who she lands and who she keeps. Stand where I can read you. I am going to tell you some things you will wish you had not come down to hear, and you had better hear them on your feet."

choices.label: Ask him what he means, who she keeps
Ask him who set this crossing
Refuse to be read; tell him you came for a person, not a verdict

blocks.s: "What does he decide." The stoker considers his cold shovel. "Who she keeps. That's the post. The Captain reads the manifest and he says who's landed and who's kept, and what he says, the sea does. You'd think a man like that would be a brute about it. He isn't. That's the worst of him. He's fair. He'll sit you down and tell you exactly why she's keeping you, and it'll be true, and you'll near enough thank him."
He shifts his grip on the shovel, the only movement in him.

"I'll tell you the one thing nobody up top will. He doesn't keep the worthless ones. The worthless ones he lands; lets them surface, sends them off into the morning to grow old and rot like everyone ashore. It's the good ones he keeps. The ones worth keeping. That's his whole trade. What the sea finds worth keeping, she keeps below, and she does not give it back." The shovel grates an inch in the clinker. "So don't go up there hoping to be found wanting. Down here, wanting is the only ticket up."

choices.label: Ask about the count
Go to the manifest desk
Go up to the catwalk

blocks.s: You ask him, plainly, who set this crossing. Whose night this is. He looks at you a long moment, weighing whether you can carry it, the way a master weighs whether the sea will take more sail.

"This crossing was set for you," he says. "Not stumbled into. Set. Laid on like a special sailing, at the asking of someone who could not bear the morning that was coming. I will not say a name; names are your affair, and you have crossed a whole ship trying to remember one. But there was a loss that the morning was going to finish. Someone time was going to take, or you, taken from someone. And one of you, the one whose loss you fled, could not face the far side of that morning, and so a crossing was asked for, and granted, and I sailed it." The pen scratches, once, in the margin. "A night that never finishes, so the morning never comes, so the loss never lands. That is the whole of what this ship is. A held breath, at one soul's asking, so that the thing that was going to be taken never quite is."
He lets that sit in the cold.

"You came down here thinking you chose to flee. You did. But the ship was waiting, set and ready, because someone needed you not to have to wake into that morning. The love was real. It built you a sea to hide the dawn behind. That is not the same as it being kind."

choices.label: Ask what the held night actually cost
Refuse to hear the rest; turn for the ladder down

blocks.s: You put your shoulder to the cold steel edge beside hers and you push, and the two of you together check it, just, the door grinding to a stop an inch off her heels and no more, and you both stand there braced against it, hearing the count come up the deck, the bosun at his lever, a number and a number.

"Right," she says, breathing hard, all the armour off it now. "Right. On three you let your side go and I go through and you keep yours held a half-beat so it doesn't take my foot. You've been seen to do this sort of thing before, haven't you. You've that look. The look that's still in time."

You have, in fact, been seen. Somewhere above on this ship someone saw the living time-bound you under the drowned calm and named it, and the door knows it, and the steel gives that half-inch a soul gets when the ship has had to admit it cannot quite keep one. You count her through. She goes, scrambling, soaked, and turns in the gap on the far side, lit by the bulkhead lamp, entirely her own size, no calm on her at all.

"There," she says, and then, recovering, dry: "God, I'm wet. Best I've ever felt." She does not thank you, which is the highest thing she could do. "A person got out tonight. Keep that. You'll want it where you're going, down at the water. Go on. I'll find a ladder that stays a ladder. I always said I'd leave by a door that shut after."

choices.label: Go down to the lever and the bosun, lighter

blocks.s: You cross the cold ash to the stokehold, where the furnaces stand open-mouthed and black, and a stoker is at his place before the middle furnace, leaning on his shovel, the blade down in a heap of clinker gone to stone. He is stripped to a singlet grey with old coal, and he is not cold, or has stopped being able to tell, and he is not stoking, because there is nothing here to stoke.

On the plating beside him a stoker's enamel mug stands, white once, chipped to the black iron at the rim, and it is welded to the deck. Not set down. Welded, the cold come up through the plating and taken the last of the heat out of the dregs and fixed the mug to the steel as if it had grown there. He sees you looking.

"Aye, that's mine," he says, not moving. "Set it down a minute. Hundred year ago, give or take. You learn not to set things down, down here." He tips his head toward the catwalk above without lifting his eyes to it. "Captain'll see you. He always sees you. It's the one thing down here that still works."

choices.label: Ask him what the Captain decides
Ask him about the count you keep hearing
Go on to the manifest desk
Go up to the catwalk and the Captain

146.title: The Door That Shut on Her (an ending)

blocks.s: You came down without the thing that would have let you help her. No one saw the living time-bound you under the calm and named it; you handed that away somewhere above, to a cup or a chair or a kindness, and so when you put your shoulder to the closing door beside hers the steel does not give the half-inch a soul gets when the ship has had to admit it cannot quite keep one. It only closes, slow, certain, an inch and an inch, and the two of you cannot check it, and you both know it before either of you says so.

"It's all right," she says, which is the worst thing she could say, because she does not mean it and she is saying it for you. "You tried. Most don't even try. Get your foot clear, go on, I'll not have it take your foot as well." The three notes, low, no shake left in them, which is worse than the shake. The door grinds the last of the gap shut between you, her face going to a line and then to steel, and you hear the count come up the deck, the bosun throwing nothing, the door closing on its own because the ship had her measure and you had nothing to add to the scale.

You go down to the breach the way you came, with the way up still in your hand, but lighter by something you will feel the lack of in the water: the proof that a person can stay in time, that someone got out tonight. She did not. You were the one who could have weighed the scale and you came down without the weight, and you will carry her three notes down into the dark, low and unshaking and shut away behind steel, the whole long way to the choice.

You keep refusing, and you refuse from the warm soft place, not the awake one; you have not kept your count, you let the night take it somewhere above, and so when you stand at the catwalk and tell the Captain his pencil is none of your business, you tell him in the voice of someone the ship has already half got. You make, you think, a fine defiance of it. It is, you understand too late, the most worth-keeping thing you have done all night, and your audience is the one soul on this ship that decides who is kept.
He hears all of it, patient, the pen resting in his old hand. When you are done he does not argue.

"A spirited refusal," he says, dry, and not unkindly. "The trouble with refusing the verdict in front of the man who keeps it is that the refusing is the truest reading I get of you, and this one reads well. Defiant. Whole. Worth keeping." The pen moves at last, and fills the blank beside your name that has gone unfilled this crossing and the last and the one before, one word you do not get to see, and he closes the book on it with a small soft finality. "There. You wanted to be no business of mine. You have made yourself my best business in a hundred years. She keeps what she finds worthy, and I find you worthy, and so she keeps you, below, in the night, with the rest of the good ones. You may go down to the water now. You will find it does not let you through. That is the difference between landing and being kept; the kept ones reach the breach and the breach simply holds them, like a held note, forever, found worthy, and never let up nor let down."

"Who she keeps." He turns the log a quarter-inch square to the desk, an exact man. "What the sea finds worthy. Understand me, because this is the part every soul gets backwards. I do not keep the poor crossings. The poor ones I land; I let them surface into the grey morning and grow old and lose everything by inches, which is the common lot, and a mercy, though none of them ever thank me for it. The good ones, the worth-keeping ones, those I keep. Below. In the night. Forever. A thing worth keeping is not a thing you let go up into the weather to be spoiled."
The pen taps once against the log, the way a slow clap is a clap held to one beat.

"That is the sea's appetite, and I am its hand. I keep what is worth keeping. I make no apology for it; an appetite that apologises is just a hunger pretending to manners." A dry breath. "You are afraid I will find you wanting. You have it exactly upside down. Down here, to be found wanting is the only way back up the ladder. Pray I am not impressed."

choices.label: Ask who set this crossing, then
Ask what he is, under the coat

blocks.s: You go past him to the last ladder, and the bosun holds the door, his hand off the iron and his count held still, and below you for the first time on this whole ship there is a sound of water moving, a slow cold working at the plates, the sea coming in at last where the keel is breached, where the held night finally gives out and the real and final dark begins.

You are not the one who came down the first ladder an hour ago. You came down with the way up in your hand and you have it still, and now you have the rest of it too: the Captain's verdict, that what the sea finds worthy it keeps below forever, and that to be let up you must be willing to be spoiled; the cost named and witnessed, the child frozen at the door of the morning that never came, and everyone you held safe held with it; the struck name in the book, the one who built a sea to hide a dawn behind and is on no list because it cannot follow you down here nor up into the light. You go down knowing all of it, which does not make the choice below easier. It makes it true.

Behind and above, the Captain's lamp is a small steady point on the catwalk, and the pen does not move, and you do not know, and may never know, whether he filled in your verdict or struck the blank through. From the warm far above, faint, a slow clap comes once and stops.

lines: THE CURATOR, close now, and for the first time not entirely steady, rising up the last of the dark to meet you:

Down to the breach at last, where every passenger must finally stand alone in the water. You went and you looked at it; I felt you do it; almost no one does, and it changes the cold. I will not pretend I am glad. The ones who look are the hardest to keep and the hardest to land, and I was never any good at the second of those. I built a sea once, you know, to hide a morning behind; I never did reach the far shore of it; that is the whole of what I am and the one thing I may not say. Go down. The water is waiting, and so, for as long as you like, so am I, who have rather too much of the for-as-long-as-you-like, and no breach of my own to go down to.

blocks.s: The ladder takes you down toward the moving water, and the breach, and the choice that the cold dark below is at last allowed to ask.
You ask the Captain what it is, the child in the cold, though some part of you has already stopped needing the answer.

"It is the morning that never came," he says, quiet, the lamp steady in his old hand. "Every held night costs one. You stopped the clock at twenty past two so the dawn would never break, so the loss would never finish, and a dawn that never breaks is a day that never happens, and a day that never happens is a child that never wakes into it. Not grown. Not lost. Not even properly here. Just never let to arrive." The pen-hand is empty now, no pen, no clap, and that is its own kind of mercy. "That is what the safety cost. Not the morning of the loss alone. All the mornings after it. The whole long future that was waiting in the day you would not walk into, frozen at the age it was when you stopped the clock, kept down here in the cold where the held night keeps what it took to be held."
The balloon taps the iron, dull, once.
"I do not show you this to wound you. I show you because you cannot weigh the breach if you do not know what staying keeps frozen, and what leaving lets thaw. Go down to it, or do not. But look."

choices.label: Go down into the cold to the child
You cannot bear it; turn back

blocks.s: He leads you aft of the dead boilers to where the plating steps down once more, to a low compartment between the engines and the after bulkhead, and the cold here is the coldest in the ship, the cold of the open sea come all the way in. The black skin of water on the floor has gone to ice at the edges. The lamp the Captain holds throws one reach of yellow into it and no further.
In that reach, sat on a coil of cold chain because the legs do not reach the floor, there is a child.

You knew there would be. You have caught the edge of it the whole way down, the small shape at the porthole, the tin boat in the steward's coat, the question read off a moving mouth through glass. Knowing did not ready you. The child sits very still in the cold, not crying, not lost, not drowned, only still, the way a thing is still that was never wound. Frozen at an age, a young age, the age you were the day before the morning came, the age before the loss. A balloon is tied to the chain beside it, a child's foil balloon gone dull and slack, and it drifts low in the cold and taps, once, against the iron.
The Captain lowers the lamp and says nothing, for once, and lets the cold do the work.

choices.label: Go down into the cold to the child
Ask the Captain what it is
You cannot. Turn back rather than feel this

blocks.s: The way down to the breach runs past the bosun, and he is where the stoker said, at a great cleated lever set in a slotted iron gate, the watertight seal, the one that throws the last door between the engines and the cold water below. He is a big man gone still as the machinery, his hand resting on the iron, and he is counting, low, under his breath, cracking one knuckle between the numbers.

"Forty," he says, to the lever, not to you. "You'll be the one he read. They come down past me after, to go to the water." Fifty. The knuckle. "This here's the seal. I throw it, the door drops, and everything this side of it is held in the night for good, engines and souls and the warm and you, sealed where nothing more can be taken. Sixty." He looks at his own hand on the iron, ashamed of how well it fits there. "I've thrown it. I've not thrown it. Depends on the night, and on whether the one in front of me's worth the not-throwing, and on whether I can keep my count past the wanting. Seventy. And I do want it. That's the trouble with a big quiet man and a quiet ship. She found the part of me that likes a thing held down and a door shut hard, and built me a whole watch out of it. Eighty."

choices.label: Ask him not to throw it; you mean to go down on your own feet
Tell him you do not know what you want; let him decide
Ask him to throw it; you have seen the cost and you choose the night

blocks.s: He does the rare thing then, the thing he has not done since you came aboard. He stops finishing your words and reaches, slow, for one of his own, and you can see it cost him, a strain in a flat voice that has not strained in a hundred crossings.

"I'm going to send a true message," he says. "Mine. Not yours, not the position, not the same word I've tapped into the dark since I forgot which shore I sent from. A true one. It takes from me to do it; there's not much true left in me to spend and I'll be the less for it after. So hear it once." The key moves under his hand, slow, deliberate, each letter paid for. "The one who keeps this show. Who pours the welcome and seats you in the warm and cannot ever bring the night to an end. Ask why a thing builds an endless crossing and never lands. Ask what such a one paid, and what it paid in, that it cannot follow you down to the breach nor up into the morning, that it is the one soul in all these houses with no shore coming. It is on no list because it struck itself off every list, long ago, to buy its way out of an ending it could not face. I can't say more. There isn't more in me. But you'll not be able to think it's only the one who runs the ship, after this."
The key stops. The headphone slips from his ear and he does not lift it back. Somewhere in the set, very faint, there is a sound like a thing that has paid for speaking and is resting.

choices.label: Leave him to his rest and go up to the catwalk

blocks.s: You will not be read. You tell him so, that you did not come the whole long way down to have a man with a pen tell you what you are worth, that you came for a person and a morning and not for a verdict, that he can keep his judgement.
He does not raise his voice. He picks the pen back up.

"You misunderstand the post," he says, dry. "I do not read you to your face for sport. I read you because a soul unjudged cannot be entered in the book, and a soul unentered drifts about the engine room getting under the machinery. You may refuse the verdict. You may not refuse to have one. An idle hand fills in a blank, and my hand is on the pen, and the blank beside your name is the one thing on this ship that has gone unfilled, this crossing and the last and the one before." He looks at you with the flat patience of a thing that has heard every refusal a frightened soul can make. "Stand and hear what you are weighing, and go down to the breach knowing it. Or refuse, and I will mark you for what refusing makes you, and keep you below for it, which is a commoner and a duller verdict than you would like."

choices.label: Stand, and hear what the held night cost
Keep refusing; let the pen do what it will
Refuse, but you are awake and counting; hold his eye

blocks.s: The stair from the engine room runs out at last, and there is no more down to go; you have come to the lowest place she has, the bottom of her, the cold dark inside the keel where the working lamps give out and the only light is the grey that comes off the water itself. The breach is here. It is not a great tearing wound, nothing so loud; it is a long seam started low in the plating where the cold black sea has finally been let in, and it stands across the deck-plates an inch deep and rising, level and patient, the same water that has stood all night at the same step on the grand staircase, come down here to meet you where it can.

No band reaches this far. No warmth. There is the iron groan of a ship that has been held from finishing and wants, all down her length, to finish; there is the small lap of the sea against the steel; there is your own breath, which down here at last begins to thin and show, a pale thing in the cold.

You carry the way up with you, the ladder the purser showed you that works both ways, and you carry what you witnessed in the engine room, the frozen child and the morning that never came. You did not arrive here clean. You arrived here knowing.

You look down at the black water at your feet, and your face is in it, giving back the grey, and you go to lift your wrist out of old habit to check the hour, and the face in the water lifts its wrist a half-beat after you, not with you, late, the way a thing answers when it is reading you rather than being you. And the face down there is calm. Flat calm, the drowned calm of the long-aboard, even now, even here, while your own face up in the cold is anything but. You put your arm down. The water keeps your face a moment longer than it should before it lets it go.

choices.label: Go straight to the water at the breach and the reckoning
Find the company first, gathered at the edge of the dark
Steady yourself; pay out the sounding line into the breach
Look for the girl, if you brought her down with you

blocks.s: You take the ladder. You set your hand on the wet rung the purser is holding steady, and then the next, and you climb, up out of the water, up off the bottom of her, and the moment you are clear the breach is allowed at last to do its work, and behind you and below you the long held sinking begins, the ship let go into the thing she has wanted all night, the water coming up the deck-plates in a rush that is almost relief.

You climb. The cold comes down the rungs harder the higher you go, the real cold, the outside cold off a sea that is allowed to move, and your breath tears at it, and your hands ache on the steel, and every rung is a thing you have to choose again because the warm is below you now, calling soft, and the morning above you is only cold and grey and full of everything you ran from.

Below you, in the dark water going up the plates, the calm drowned face turns up to watch you climb. It does not reach for you. It does not climb after you. It only watches, content, the one you stopped being, left in the night so the one you might still become can go up into the cold and the years. The collar is turned under wrong, the side you never turn it. You climb past where you can see it. You do not look back down.

choices.label: Climb out, into the grey morning and all the years

blocks.s: You ask him what it costs, to hold a ladder that would let his ship lose the one soul she came all this way to keep. The case clicks.

"What it costs." He considers the wet rung under his hand. "A purser is reckoned by a full ship, every berth boarded, the manifest closed and complete. Every soul that goes up this ladder is a berth I did not keep. She knows it. She is patient with me about it the way she is patient with everything, which is to say she waits for me to forget I ever held a rung steady at all, and mostly I do forget, between crossings, and have to learn it back."
He is quiet a moment, the wet creeping up his sleeve.

"I keep a thing in my coat so as not to forget. I told you once I would not say what. I will say this much of it now: it floats. It is the one thing I own that the sea cannot keep down, and I hold a ladder for the same reason I keep it dry." The case clicks. "Go and stand at the water. Whatever you do, I have a rung ready, and that is the whole of my office, and for once it might be for sending someone up."

choices.label: Go to the choice at the breach

158.title: Your Own Name, Out Loud (an ending)

blocks.s: There is one more thing you could do, and at the water, with the held night listening, you do it, because it seems like the bravest thing and because no one warned you sharply enough that it is not. You say your own name. Not the name off the card, the true one, the short proud one a child once held tight as a tin boat, and after it, before you can think better of it, the year, the real year, the one it ought to be ashore where the clocks run.

The night hears it. Of course it does. It hears everything. But a true name and a true year, said aloud at the breach, are not a way out; they are the surest keeping there is, because now the night knows exactly which soul came down to the water and refused to be ordinary about it, the living one, the original, the one who came up out of running time and remembers the dock. The captain's pen moves, once, a small satisfied stroke. The water knows your name now, and a sea that knows your name does not let it go.

The cold does not lift. The hour does not change. You named yourself in the one place where being the original is the trap and not the escape, and the held night leans in, almost grateful, to keep you forever now as the rarest thing in its manifest: the living one, on record, at the breach, who told the sea its own true name and the year, out loud, and was kept for it.

You do not climb and you do not lie down and you do not refuse; you simply stop fighting the cold, the way a tired swimmer stops treading water, and let the breach have you where you stand. It is not a decision. It is the absence of one, dressed up by the night as rest.

The water comes up your legs and it is not cold any more, it has gone warm, the night seeing to that, glad, attentive, and the warmth climbs you gentle as a tide and you let it, and let it, and somewhere in the letting the reason you came down here goes quietly out of you, a name you have stopped using. There was a ladder you could have climbed. A morning you came back for. A person you stopped being, just there under the surface, that you meant to reclaim or leave on purpose, one or the other, awake. You had the whole choice in your hands. You set it down to free up the hands for nothing at all.

The night takes you in and holds you, unhurried, the one who chose nothing, kept under the kindest water there is, the warm closing over you the way warm water closes over a held breath, and somewhere far above the band turns over the same bar it never finishes, and you do not hear the loss in it any more, because you are going under now, content, drowned calm at last, and there was never any bottom under her, and you go down past where the marks run out, into the deep that gives back nothing.

You do not take the ladder. You turn your back on the wet rungs and the cold coming down them, and you let the warmth come back up around you, the whole sealed length of her, the band turning over far above, the held hour settling over you soft as the water settling over the deck-plates.

You lie down in the held night. There is no other way to say it that is honest. You let yourself down into the cold water at the breach that is not cold any more, that has gone warm and close and kind, and the person you stopped being is here, just here, under the surface at the same step, not gone, never gone, and you lie down beside them in the stopped hour and let the held night close over the both of you.

Nothing more can be taken from you now. That is what you have bought, all of it, and it is exactly what you came aboard for, and it is real. The morning will not come. The loss in it will not finish. You will lie here, safe and stopped and dead-still, with the dying beside you and never going, for as long as twenty past two will last.

choices.label: Let the held night close over you, lulled, the stillness all the way in
Stay, but with your eyes open, knowing exactly what it costs

blocks.s: You let the held night close over you, and the stillness comes all the way in, and it is, you have to say it honestly, a relief past anything the cold morning could ever have given you. The water at the breach goes warm. The groan of the ship eases, because you have stopped asking her to finish, and she settles back into the held hour grateful as you are, and the band far above takes up the same bar it never finishes, and you are home.

The person you stopped being is here beside you in the stopped water, and they are not gone, and now they never will be, because gone only happens in a morning and there is no morning here. You lie beside them in the warm dark at the same step the water has stood at all night, and you feel the count go out of you, the last insisting part that knew the hour was a held thing, snuffed soft as a galley light at the end of a long watch, and you stop minding that it is always twenty past two, and then you stop minding anything at all.

Nothing more will ever be taken from you. You came aboard for exactly this and you have it now, for good: the safe stopped night, the dying beside you and never going, the loss held off forever an inch short of finishing, the morning standing off out there past the hull and never let in. It is everything you wanted. It is a kind of death, lain down in willingly, with someone you love kept beside you past the point of keeping, and it is so warm, and so still, and so safe, that there is no longer anyone in you awake enough to tell that the warmth and the stillness and the safety are all the water is now, closing over you gently, by degrees, in the held hour that does not end.

You tell him you do not want his verdict. You did not come down a whole ship to be told which door is the worthy one so that whichever you take the choosing was never yours. You will go to the water with no judgment on you. You watch it cost him.

The pen stops over the log. For a long moment he does not lift it, the line unfinished, and something in the still exact face goes slack at an edge, because a captain who is not allowed to pass a verdict on a soul standing in front of him is a captain holding a wheel that has come off its shaft.

"As you wish," he says at last, and there is no withering in it now, only the fineness worn down to something tired. "I will set nothing down on you. You will go to the water unjudged, unkept, no entry against your name in my book. It is the one mercy I have it in me to give, and it is the one I was built specifically not to give, and you have asked me for it, and I find I would rather pay it than not." The pen does not move. "Go down. I will not watch to see which door. A captain does not get to know how it comes out, when he has agreed not to keep it."

choices.label: Go down to the choice at the breach

blocks.s: The purser is at the foot of the ladder, his dark coat black with the wet to the waist, and the watch-case clicks once under his cuff before he speaks, a half-beat ahead of him, the same as it sounded at the head of the gangway a whole ship ago.

"This is the ladder, then," he says, clipped, low, all the old courtesy gone thin over the tired thing. "I have spent every crossing this ship ever made bringing the company down. Down the gangway, down the stair, down to the warm, and down here at the last to the water. That is my post, the bringing down. I never once, in all of it, sent a soul back up."
He sets his hand flat on the wet rung, not lifting it for you yet, only holding it steady.

"A boat works both ways. So does a ladder. The drowned come down it; one living soul could go up it. They do not put that in the manifest. I am telling you, the way I told you to mind the sill." The case clicks. "I will hold it for you, if you climb. I cannot climb it; my post is the bringing down, and a man does not get to leave the thing he is for. But I can hold a rung steady for one who can. Mind your hands. The cold has teeth on the wet steel."

choices.label: Ask what it costs him to hold a ladder that empties his ship
Go on to the choice at the breach

blocks.s: You turn and look back up the way you came, up the working dark toward the warm, and you let yourself feel, honestly, what staying would keep, because it will not be honest to choose against a thing you have refused to look at.

It would keep all of it. The band on the held bar, never the last. The cup warm in your hands and never quite finished. The morning held off forever, and so the loss in it held off forever, because the loss only finishes in a morning and there is no morning here. Everyone you came aboard with, still aboard. The person you came for, not gone, never gone, lying just there under the water at the same step, kept, beside you, for good. Nothing more ever taken. No year you cannot walk into, because there is no next year, there is only this one held hour, twenty past two, safe and warm and stopped and yours.

It is not a small thing they are offering. It would be easy to draw it as a trap and feel clever for seeing through it, but standing here in the cold with your breath gone to vapour you can feel exactly how much of it is real mercy: to never lose anyone again. You did not come down here because the offer was thin. You came down here because it is the most generous thing anyone has ever held out to you, and you have to decide anyway.

choices.label: Let the warmth come back up around you; go toward staying
Pull yourself off it; go to the face in the water
Put your hand on the ladder up instead

blocks.s: You reach for the ladder. There is no ladder. You never found the rung the purser would have held; you came down to the water without the way up, and now at the last, with the cold climbing you and the ship going down around you, your hands go out for a thing to climb that is not there, the way a drowning hand goes out for a boat, and there is no boat, because you never earned one, and the dark where the rungs should be gives back nothing but wet plating and the rising sea.

You claw for it anyway. That is the worst of it, the clawing, the body's plain animal want to live going out into the dark for a ladder you did not find when you had the chance, finding the cold steel of the hull and no rung, no rope, no hand, only the seam of the breach widening and the black water coming up over the reach of your fingers.

The sounding line told you. No bottom under her. You feel the deck-plates go from under your feet and there is nothing below them, never was, only the deep, and you go into it reaching, the cold taking the reach last of all, your hand still out for the boat that was never there, the marks running out past you in the dark, fathom after fathom, into the deep that gives back nothing and never did.

The girl is here, the newest aboard, the one you helped keep her head above the water back in the engine room, and she is still herself, soaked through and shaking with the cold, her teeth going, the lent frock ruined, and she is keeping her count, three rising notes under her breath, the marks of the hours penciled up the inside of her wrist where the ship was never let to see.

"You actually came down here," she says, and her voice shakes and she will not let it. "Mad. Completely mad. There is a perfectly good warm ship up there and you came down to stand in the bilge in the dark with a sinking. Some people."
She hugs herself, and the three notes shake at the top, and she steadies them on purpose.

"Listen. If you go up. Send a postcard, yeah? I hear the morning's overrated. Grey, wet, everything still to be lost, getting older toward it." The dry tilt, the armour, over the fear. "Sounds bloody marvellous. I kept my count the whole way down so I would still know it was a thing being done to me and not just how it is. You keep yours. Whatever you do down there at the water, keep your own count. Do not let them tell you it never happened."

choices.label: Tell her you will keep the count; go to the choice

gated.aside: She gives you the three notes one more time, steady now, not shaking at the top, because you kept her above the water, and a person who can stay in time is the whole of the argument that a different ending is possible.

blocks.s: You come to the edge of the breach, and there is no more company and no more ladder-rungs to ask after and no more of the ship between you and the thing itself. Just the cold black water, an inch deep and rising, the same water that stood all night at the same step, come down here at last to be decided.

The ship groans the whole length of her, wanting to finish. You can feel how badly she wants it, how long she has been held off the ending, and you understand for the first time that the held night is not a kindness she does; it is a thing being done to her, the same as to you, a finishing refused.

lines: THE CURATOR, and the warmth has thinned right down, and what is under it is wistful, the voice of one who has crossed and crossed and never landed:

Here we are, then, at the water, with no more ship to walk. You may let her finish, and go up, and come out alone into the cold morning and every morning after, where the loss you fled will be waiting for you, complete, and you will age toward your own. Or you may stay, here, in the held hour, where it never finishes and nothing is ever taken again. I will not pretend I do not want you to stay. I have wanted everyone to stay. I made this crossing once myself, and I never did reach the far shore, and the morning has stood off out there waiting for me longer than you can hold in your head. Choose. I will hold the night either way, for as long as you like, which is to say always.

blocks.s: Three things wait on you. You can let her finish and surface into time. You can stay aboard in the night forever. Or you can refuse the wager itself, the surfacing and the staying both, on terms that were never the Curator's.

choices.label: Take the ladder up; let her finish; surface into the morning
Lie down in the held night; stay aboard forever
Refuse the wager entirely, both doors of it
Say your own true name and the year aloud, to claim yourself
Stop fighting the cold; let the water close over you where you stand
Reach for the ladder you never found a rung of

gated.aside: You let go once and it did not kill you, and so you tell yourself you could let go of this, the safe night, the held hour. But letting go of a stopped clock means letting time start, and time only runs one way, toward the morning and all that the morning takes.
You have never been good at letting a thing finish. The night is right here and it never ends and everyone you came aboard to keep is still aboard. No one would blame you. No one down here ever does.
You chose the cold daylight before, as your plain unloved self. You know exactly what waits up that ladder, which is the one reason your hand might not take it now, and the one reason it might.
You kept the part before. Keeping is what you do. The night is only one more thing to keep, and you are so very good at not putting a thing down.

You refused me twice now. There is a door here for that too, the one that neither climbs nor stays, and I have never once seen what is on the far side of it, which is the truest thing I will tell you all night.

blocks.s: You stay. But you will not let them warm it for you, and you will not let the count go out, and so you lie down in the held water with your eyes open and your own watch still ticking in your pocket and the marks of the hours still going up your arm, and you stay anyway, clear-eyed, knowing exactly what it is and choosing it with the knowing in.

It is worse than the lulled stay and it is more honest. You do not get the mercy of forgetting. You will lie here in the stopped hour beside the one you came for, kept, safe, with nothing more ever taken, and you will know, every held minute of it, that it is a death you climbed down into on purpose, that the morning is standing off out there and you turned your back on it, that the warmth is water and the safety is drowning slowed to a held bar. You keep your count. You keep your name. You keep the knowing.

It does not save you. It was never going to save you; you did not stay to be saved. You stay because the loss in the morning was a thing you could not walk into, and you would rather lie here knowing, beside someone not yet gone, than go up into the cold and let the river take them and you both. You chose the stopped night with your eyes open, and your eyes stay open, and that is the whole of the difference, and it is a real difference, and it is not enough to make it anything but a grave you are awake inside.
You hold there, in the rising water, in neither door, and you let the night break against it.

And it does break. Not into rescue, nothing so kind as that, and not into drowning either; it breaks the way a held note breaks when the player simply stops, into a stillness that has nowhere to go. The ship groans and cannot finish, because you have not let her, and cannot hold, because you have not let her do that either. The water stands at your knees and neither climbs nor falls, caught, like the breath of the whole ship, on a soul that will not finish crossing and will not be kept.

The purser's hand comes off the rung; this is not a going-up. The big man's hand stays on the lever with nothing to slam it for. The captain's pen hangs over the log with no verdict it can set down, because a verdict needs a passage made and you have refused to make one. And the Curator's voice, when it comes, is not the master of ceremonies any more.

lines: THE CURATOR, and there is no crossing left in it, only the bare sound of the first soul that ever came aboard and could not bear to leave, speaking at last almost as a person:

Then what is to become of us. If you will not go up and you will not stay down. I made this crossing because I could not face the morning, and I have held the night so long I forgot there was ever a shore. What am I to do with a passenger who will neither land nor drown.

blocks.s: You do not answer. You do not know the answer, and you are done handing the night lines it can use. You stand in the cold water that has stopped rising, in the held hour that has stopped being able to hold you, in neither door, and you wait out whatever comes next, on terms that were never the Curator's, and what comes is not written anywhere on this ship, and that, for the first time since the gangway, is the only thing in the place that is yours.

You go to the big man in the dark, and he has his hand on the watertight lever, the long iron arm that would slam the breach shut and seal her and hold the night for good, and he is not throwing it, and you can see what it is costing him not to, the whole arm of him shaking with the held weight of a thing he badly wants to do.

"One," he says, low, to the lever, not to you, and cracks a knuckle. "Two. The ship found the thing in me that wants a door shut hard. Built me a watch where shutting it is the whole job. I have wanted to throw this lever the entire crossing. Three. Shut you in. Hold the warm. Keep the lot of us where nothing more gets taken."
The arm shakes. He does not throw it.

"But that is forcing her to hold," he says. "That is me deciding for you, the brute on the watch slamming the door so the soft ones do not have to choose. Four. I will not. I have got my hand on it and I am keeping my hand on it precisely so no other hand can, and that is the only discipline I have got, the not-throwing." He cracks the knuckle. "Go and choose. I will hold the door open by holding the lever myself. That is the watch. Hold the door. ... Clear."

choices.label: Leave him to the lever; go to the choice

blocks.s: You will not climb the ladder and you will not lie down in the water, because both of those are answers to the question the ship asked, and you have come all the way down to the bottom of her to notice that the question is rigged: surface-alone-into-all-the-years or stay-stopped-and-safe-forever are the only two doors a thing that cannot bear to finish would ever build, and you did not come down a whole drowning ship to choose between two doors hung in the same wall by the same hand that could never walk through either.

You stand in the rising water and you do not climb and you do not lie down. You stop crossing at all. The ship groans, wanting to finish, and the warmth leans in, wanting you to stay, and you give neither of them the thing it is built to take, and the held hour, for the first time, feels less like the night holding you and more like the night not knowing what to do with a soul that will neither finish nor be kept.

lines: THE CURATOR, and there is no theatre left in it now, only the bare frightened sound of the first one ever to come aboard and be unable to leave, speaking almost as a person:
That is not one of the doors. There is no passage written for that. You cannot simply, on my crossing, decline to make it.

blocks.s: "I can," you say, to the dark water, in your own flat ordinary voice, no name in it, no year, no line it can use. "Watch me."

choices.label: Hold there, in neither door, and let the night break against it

blocks.s: They are here, the ones who have walked the crossing with you, gathered at the edge of the working dark where the grey off the water gives them just enough light to be seen by. They have not come to stop you. There are no hands on you. They have come up at the end, a crew on deck for the last of it, to be where the thing happens, each of them holding the one small post the ship left them.

The purser stands nearest the ladder. The captain is a stillness up on the half-flooded grating above, where the bridge of this lowest deck would be. The wireless man sits against a stanchion with the dead key in his lap. The big man has his hand on a lever in the dark, the watertight lever that would seal the breach and hold the night, and he is not throwing it. And there is a girl with him, if you freed her, teeth going, still herself, keeping her count.
This is the last of them you will get. After this there is only the water and the choice. Go to them now, one at a time, or go straight on to the edge of the breach and make your reckoning.

choices.label: Go to the purser and the ladder
Go up to the captain on the grating
Go to the wireless man with the dead key
Go to the big man at the lever
Go to the girl keeping her count
Leave them; go to the choice at the breach

blocks.s: You go to the wireless man, sat against the stanchion with the dead Marconi key in his lap, the wet to his thighs, the headphones round his neck gone silent. He does not finish your sentence this time. You wait for it, the half-beat-early word in your own voice, and it does not come, and the not-coming is the loudest thing he has ever said to you.

"...does not come," he says, eventually, slow, flat, and even that is late, dragged up from somewhere far down. "No. I am spent. I sent the last true message I had up in the engine room, to help you, and a man only has the one, down here. After it there is no traffic left in the hands. I cannot reach ahead of you any more. I can barely reach myself."
He turns the dead key over once, a thing that used to be the whole of him.

"I sent it for you, the true one. A position. Not the ship's. A name that was on no list, never landed, never drowned, the one who made this crossing first and worked a passage out of ever finishing it, and so keeps all of us so beautifully because it has no shore of its own to make for." The flat voice thins. "I sent it and it cost me the last of my own call-sign, and I would do it again, because a man ought to spend himself on one true thing before the sea takes the rest. Go to the water. I have nothing left to send ahead of you. You are on your own word now, and that is as it should be."

choices.label: Leave him the dead key; go to the choice

blocks.s: You put your hand on the bottom rung. It is wet steel, cold enough to ache, real in a way almost nothing on this ship has let itself be, the rust of it gritty under your palm, a thing made to be climbed by men in a hurry on a night that was allowed to end.

The purser showed you this. A boat works both ways; a ladder rigged to bring the drowned down can carry one living soul up. You can feel, with your hand on it, that it is true, that the rungs go up and up into the cold and that if you climbed them you would come out at the top into the open night air and the breach below you would be allowed, at last, to do its work, and the ship would finish her long sinking with you climbing clear of her, up, out, alone, into whatever grey morning is standing off out there past the held one, waiting to be let in.

The top of the ladder is lost in the dark. You cannot see the morning from here. You can only feel the cold coming down the rungs, the real cold, the outside cold, the cold off a sea that is allowed to move.

choices.label: Hold the rung; go on to the company at the edge
Kneel first to the face in the water

blocks.s: You go to the breach and stand at the edge of the rising water, your boots in the cold of it, and you make yourself look at what is laid out here, because everything you can do is here, present, in the dark, and the ship is not going to spell it out for you.

The way up is here: a steel ladder going up the side of the breach into the dark, rungs wet, the bottom of it standing in the water and the top of it lost up toward the cold deck and, somewhere above all of it, the open night and the morning that the night has been holding off. The purser showed you this ladder. It works both ways. You could climb it.

The held night is here too, behind and above you, the whole warm sealed length of her, the band still turning over the same bar, the deck chairs warm, the cabins turned down, all of it waiting to close over you again soft as water over a held breath, where nothing is ever asked and nothing is ever taken and the morning never comes.

And the person you stopped being is here, in the dark water at your feet, in the calm drowned face that answers you a half-beat late. To go up is to leave them in the night. To stay is to lie down beside them in it. There is no reading on the dial that is not a cost.

choices.label: Put your hand on the rung of the ladder up
Look back up at the warm held night you would be leaving
Kneel to the face in the water you came back for

blocks.s: The boatswain's sounding line is coiled on a bracket by the breach, the lead still bent on, the marks in fathoms whipped into it in coloured rag, and you take it up because you want, before anything else, to know how deep the thing under you goes. You pay it out into the black seam at your feet, hand over hand, and you count the marks as they run.

Two. Five. Ten. The lead goes down through the inch of standing water and on, down through the plating that should have stopped it, down into a deep that has no business being under the keel of a ship, and the marks run out one after another into your hand, wet and cold, and there is no bottom. There is never any bottom. You knew that. You sounded it once on the boat deck a whole crossing ago and the big man told you so, and you have come all the way down to the place where the bottom ought to be and it is still not there.

You hold the wet line a moment. Then you make it fast again on its bracket, because a thing paid all the way out and never recovered is a thing you cannot take back up, and you are not ready, yet, to let go of the line.

choices.label: Go to the water at the breach and the reckoning
Go and find the company at the edge of the dark

blocks.s: You ask him for it. You stand on the half-flooded grating and you tell the captain you want his verdict, that you have wanted a worthy word your whole life and you want it now, which door is the right one, so that you can take it knowing it was judged worthy. The pen comes down to the log.

"You want me to decide," he says, dry, exact, almost kind for the length of one clause and then not. "You have stood the whole crossing hoping someone would tell you which door is the brave one, so that whichever you take, the choosing was never quite yours. I have kept a great many who asked me that. I can give you a verdict. I can tell you, right now, which exit is the worthy one, and you will believe me, because you have wanted a captain's word more than your own."
The pen scratches, slow.

And you understand, far too late, that to take the verdict at all, from this voice, on this ship, is to be kept by it; that a captain keeps what he finds worthy, and what he has found worthy is a soul that handed him the choosing. He sets the word down in the log, and it is favourable, and it is the kindest verdict he has ever passed, and it keeps you, below, at the last, one more soul the sea found worthy and the captain wrote into the manifest forever, where you wanted to be told and were, and never had to choose at all.

You look back up the working dark for the girl, because you did not come down here alone; you helped her keep her head above the water back in the engine room, and a soul you have kept above the water does not let you go down into the breach without her, mad as it is. She is on the stair behind you, soaked, shaking, her teeth going, the lent frock ruined past returning, and she is still herself, still keeping her count, the three rising notes under her breath.

"Don't look at me like that," she says. "You went down. I went down. That is how it works now, apparently. You drag a person up out of the warm and they follow you into the dark like an idiot." The notes shake at the top and she steadies them. "I am keeping my count the whole way. Somebody on this ship ought to. Go on, then. I will be at the edge with the rest of them. Try not to do anything stupid at the water. Or do. I am not your mother."
She is here, awake, in time, freed; living proof that a person can stay themselves all the way down to the bottom of the night.

choices.label: Go to the water at the breach and the reckoning
Go with her to the company at the edge

blocks.s: You climb out the top of the ladder into the open night air, and the cold takes you whole, the real cold, the cold off a sea that is moving again, and below you and behind you the ship finishes at last the long thing she was held from, going down without sound or struggle, almost grateful, the lit portholes sliding under one by one, the band cut off mid-bar, the held hour let go. You do not watch her all the way down. You are clear of her, in the water, in the cold, in the dark before the morning, alone.

You should say it plainly, while you can still feel the warm of the held night going off your skin: this does not feel like winning. The warm was real. The mercy in it was real. You have just traded a place where nothing was ever taken again for a sea that will take everything, starting now, starting with the one you left behind in the dark under the keel, the calm drowned face that watched you climb and did not climb after, the person you stopped being, who you did not save, who you cannot save, who you have left in the night so that you could go up into the cold and grow old and lose the rest.

The grey comes up. It comes up the way real mornings come, slow and indifferent and without ceremony, a paleness along an edge of the world that does not care whether you survived to see it, and you are in the cold water under it, aging already, mortal again, your breath tearing and real and counting itself out, and every year that the held night was keeping off is out there now, ahead of you, free to come, free to take, the river you stepped back into running the only way it runs. You came aboard to escape the morning after a loss. You have surfaced into every morning after that there will ever be.

Something bumps your hand in the water. It is the steward's coat, the scorched cuff, floating, and out of its pocket the child's tin sailboat has worked free at last and rides the swell beside you, the red thumbed off its keel, going nowhere, going anywhere, loose on the moving sea, and you catch it, and hold it, and let the cold morning have you, alive, exposed, mortal, and free, with all the years and all their losses standing off out there waiting to be let in, and you let them in, because that is the whole of what being in time is, and you chose it, and it is yours.

You kneel at the edge of the breach, down to where the cold water can reach your knees, and you look into it, into the face that is yours and is not, the calm one, the drowned one, the person you stopped being on the morning you ran. Up close the wrongness is small and total. The collar of the coat down there is turned under on the side you would never turn it. The wrist comes up to check a time that is not there, a half-beat after yours, late, reading you.

It does not speak. Higher up the double made its offers and trades; it is past all that now, this far down. It only looks at you with your own settled face and waits, content as the long-aboard are content, to be left or to be lifted, with no preference it will trouble you with, because it stopped wanting anything the night you ran and left it standing on a dock.
The water laps. Your two faces hang there an inch apart, one breathing cold vapour, one not breathing at all, and for a moment you genuinely cannot tell which of you is the reflection.

choices.label: Go to the company gathered at the edge of the dark

gated.aside: The face in the black water answers half a beat after yours and wears the drowned calm even while your own face does not, and you do not let yourself finish the thought about whose face it really is. Not tonight. Not down here.

blocks.s: You climb the half-flooded ladder to the grating where the captain stands, the one who set this crossing, at the need of the one whose loss you fled. He does not turn at once. There is the slow scratch of a pen in the log under his hand, a single line set down, before he speaks, and his voice is dry and exact and worn very fine.

"You came down all the way, then. Most do not. Most turn in at a warm door and I close the manifest on them and that is the verdict, and they never know one was passed." The pen scratches. "You want to know mine. You have wanted it the whole crossing, as a passenger always wants the captain's word, whether the ship is found worthy, whether you are. I keep what the sea finds worthy. I have kept a great many. They are all still aboard. You have seen where I keep them."
He looks at you, finally, and for the length of one clause the dry exactness has something almost kind under it, and then it closes.

"I can pass you a verdict. I can tell you which door is the worthy one, and you will believe me, because you have wanted a worthy word more than you have wanted your own, your whole life. Or I can withhold it, and let you go to the water with no verdict on you at all, which costs me more than you know, because a captain who passes no judgment is a captain who has stopped keeping his ship."

choices.label: Refuse the verdict; go to the water with no judgment on you
Ask him for the verdict; let him tell you which door is worthy
